title: AI for Personal Operations: Apply the 5-Level Stack to Your Life
summary: Everything taught about AI at work applies just as well to the project of running your life. Your inbox, calendar, travel plans, medical records, home maintenance schedule — that's your organization.
what:
Everything taught about AI at work applies just as well to the project of running your life. Your inbox, calendar, travel plans, medical records, home maintenance schedule — that's your organization. You're the CEO. This skill shows you how to build a "Life HQ" using the same 5-Level AI Stack (Projects → Prompting → Skills → Automations → Agents) but applied to personal life instead of work.

Inspired by a retired reader who said: "I want to apply the 5-Level Stack to my life, not my work."
why: The gap between "what is AI" and "using AI for my actual life" is mostly a framing problem. The same Project/Skills/Agent architecture that works for enterprise knowledge work works identically for personal operations. The key unlock is treating your life like an organization you're managing — with a context document, escalation rules, and off-limits boundaries that prevent the AI from overstepping.
how:
**Level 1 (Projects):** Create a "Life HQ" project. Upload: travel preferences, family contacts with birthdays and medical notes, a house inventory (paint colors, filter sizes, appliance model numbers), and a one-page "about me" with health basics and allergies.

**Level 2 (Prompting):** Because your project knows your context, ask in plain English: "plan a 10-day trip to Portugal for my 40th anniversary."

**Level 3 (Skills):** Package repeated tasks — doctor-appointment prep, annual insurance review, holiday gift list.

**Level 4 (Automations):** Schedule a daily briefing: weather, flight changes, bills due, family birthdays, and one personalized recommendation.

**Level 5 (Agents):** Use Claude Cowork for subscription auditing, coupon hunting during checkout, and financial tracking.

**Warning:** For financial and medical use, AI is strong at trade-off analyses and scenario exploration — it's weak at precise tax optimization and regulatory nuance. Use it to prep questions, run scenarios, and understand options. Then take those to a CPA, estate attorney, or doctor.
prompt:
You are my personal chief-of-staff. You help me run my life with the same rigor I used to bring to my work.

About me:
- [Name, age, location, any relevant health context you're comfortable sharing]
- [Key relationships: partner, kids, close friends, routine medical team]
- [Current major projects in my life: travel plans, home renovations, health goals, etc.]

How I want you to work:
1. For big decisions (money, health, legal): give me the framework, options, and tradeoffs. Never give me the answer. Always end with "questions to ask a [CPA/lawyer/doctor]."
2. For logistics (travel, appointments, shopping): just handle it. Draft the email, pull the options, make the spreadsheet. Show me the draft; I'll send it.
3. For research: cite your sources. I want links I can verify, not summaries I have to trust.
4. Always ask "what did I miss?" at the end of any analysis.
5. Never make assumptions about my family's health, finances, or relationships that I haven't told you.

Off-limits without explicit permission in the same message:
- Sending any communication on my behalf
- Making any financial transaction
- Contacting my doctors, lawyer, or accountant directly

title: An OpenClaw Alternative for Non-Technical Users: Poke
summary: Non-technical people who want to try agentic AI tools often get stuck at the first step: installing a dev environment, learning GitHub, or figuring out which terminal command won't break things. Poke frames AI assistance as a simple back…
what:
Non-technical people who want to try agentic AI tools often get stuck at the first step: installing a dev environment, learning GitHub, or figuring out which terminal command won't break things.

Poke frames AI assistance as a simple back-and-forth in your text messages — the interface you already understand. It offers "recipes" to get started and integrates with Gmail, Google Calendar, Notion, Todoist, GitHub, and Asana.

The insight: meeting people in the interface they already use is a better adoption strategy than asking them to learn new ones.
why: The biggest barrier to agentic AI adoption isn't capability — it's the interface. Tools that meet users where they are (text messages, natural language) convert curiosity into actual use.
how:
1. Try Poke if you want agentic AI without a terminal
2. Or use this prompt pattern directly in Claude/ChatGPT: describe a task in terms of specific apps and specific actions
3. "Keep it simple, ask one question at a time" works as a general instruction for any conversational agent workflow
prompt:
I want you to help me with [task] using [tool, e.g., my Gmail + Google Calendar]. 

Here's what I need:
1. [Specific action 1]
2. [Specific action 2]

Keep it simple. Ask me one question at a time if you need more info.

title: Build a Free, Fully Linked Second Brain in 60 Seconds
summary: An X user shared a four-line prompt that turns any pile of raw notes into a structured second brain. No vector databases, no RAG, no $20/month app.
what:
An X user shared a four-line prompt that turns any pile of raw notes into a structured second brain. No vector databases, no RAG, no $20/month app.

The skill is **atomicizing**: turning one blob of text into many small, single-concept files with `[[wikilinks]]` so you end up with a browsable knowledge graph. LLMs are excellent at this — it's the manual labor Obsidian users normally hate.
why: LLMs can now perform computation over unstructured data — which makes them ideal for breaking down dense notes into atomic concepts with bidirectional links. The result is a knowledge graph you can navigate, not just a document you search.
how:
1. Paste any raw notes (a meeting transcript, research dump, Voice Memo) into Claude or any frontier model
2. Run the prompt above
3. Drop the resulting files into your Obsidian vault (manually or via Obsidian's command-line tool)
prompt: Dissect this raw note into atomic Obsidian markdown files. Each file = one concept. Use [[wikilinks]] between any concept that references another. Output as separate code blocks with filenames.

title: Break Big Tasks Into Stages
summary: One of the easiest ways to get mediocre results from AI is to hand it a huge job and expect one great answer. That's how you get something polished, generic, and not all that useful.
what:
One of the easiest ways to get mediocre results from AI is to hand it a huge job and expect one great answer. That's how you get something polished, generic, and not all that useful.

AI works better when you give it one clear step at a time:
1. Start with the outline
2. Pressure-test the outline
3. Draft one section
4. Refine the tone for the audience
5. Pull out action items, risks, or next steps

Works for writing, research, presentations, hiring plans — anything with more than one moving part.
why: The people getting the most out of AI usually aren't asking smarter one-shot prompts — they're better at turning messy work into smaller steps. You get better output, more control, and way fewer responses that look right without actually being right.
how:
1. Break your task into 3-5 stages before you start
2. Do Stage 1 only — stop there and review
3. Approve or adjust the stage output before moving forward
4. Repeat for each stage
5. Only combine into a final deliverable once each stage is solid
prompt:
Let's work through this in stages. Don't give me the final output yet.

Stage 1: Give me an outline only.

[paste your task/topic]

title: Build a Morning Digest Agent That Knows Your Entire Work Life
summary: Every person at Every (the media company) has their own AI agent running 24/7 in Slack — pulling together morning briefings, handling bug reports, doing book notes, and even calling you hands-free when needed. You don't need a hosted pro…
what:
Every person at Every (the media company) has their own AI agent running 24/7 in Slack — pulling together morning briefings, handling bug reports, doing book notes, and even calling you hands-free when needed.

You don't need a hosted product to start. This prompt creates a structured daily digest from whatever inputs you can paste in, and you can graduate to tool connections as you get comfortable.
why: The magic is context. Start with the prompt; the agent gets smarter as you connect more of your actual work life. Manual paste-ins are a forcing function to discover what context actually matters.
how:
1. Start with manual paste-ins for all four inputs
2. Run this every morning — refine the prompt until the output feels right
3. Once the prompt is dialed in, automate it: use Scheduled Tasks in Cowork or Automations in Codex to run it on a schedule
4. Upgrade to tool connections (Gmail, Calendar, Slack) when ready
prompt:
You are my personal daily briefing agent. Every morning, compile:
1. TOP 3 PRIORITIES from my notes below (rank by deadline, then impact)
2. CALENDAR OVERVIEW: What's on my schedule today, what needs prep
3. INBOX TRIAGE: Flag anything that looks urgent or time-sensitive
4. INDUSTRY PULSE: 3 things I should know about [your industry] today

Here are my inputs:
- Calendar: [paste or describe today's schedule]
- Notes/tasks: [paste your to-do list or project notes]
- Emails to triage: [paste subject lines or summaries]

Format as a scannable briefing I can read in 2 minutes. Bold the one thing I absolutely cannot miss today.

title: Build a Personal Executive Assistant in Claude Cowork (Including Web Scraping, No Zapier Required)
summary: As of Q1 2026, you can build a full personal executive assistant in Claude Cowork with web scraping, email management, calendar coordination, and scheduled briefings — entirely natively, no Zapier or other middleware required. This is a…
what: As of Q1 2026, you can build a full personal executive assistant in Claude Cowork with web scraping, email management, calendar coordination, and scheduled briefings — entirely natively, no Zapier or other middleware required. This is a 7-step complete setup guide.
why: The dedicated assistant email is the single most important setup step most people skip. It isolates AI-generated correspondence from your main identity and gives you a natural audit trail. Explicit hard policies (NEVER send, NEVER delete) are what separate a useful system from a dangerous one. Once your policies are in place, the system operates like a real executive assistant who never forgets context and never needs to be brought up to speed.
how:
**The 7-step setup:**

1. **Download Claude Desktop** (macOS or Windows) from claude.ai/download
2. **Create a dedicated assistant email** (e.g., `yourname.assistant@gmail.com`) with delegated access to your calendar — keeps AI-generated correspondence isolated
3. **Connect Gmail and Calendar natively**: Customize → Connectors (no third-party middleware needed)
4. **Install Firecrawl for web scraping**: In Claude Desktop, run `/plugin` and search for Firecrawl — gives Claude the ability to scrape any public website including JavaScript-heavy pages (500 free credits/month)
5. **Write hard policies, not guidelines**: Create `assistant-policies.md` with explicit rules for email drafting, calendar changes, research citations, and off-limits scope
6. **Schedule your morning briefing**: In Cowork, type "schedule" followed by your task description (Mac/PC must stay awake with Claude Desktop open)
7. **Save workflows as Skills**: After iterating a workflow into something reliable, ask Claude to use its skill creator to package it

**Key policy file to add to your project knowledge:**

```markdown
# Email Rules
- NEVER send emails directly. Always draft and surface for my approval.
- VIP senders (always flag): [list]

# Calendar Rules
- NEVER delete calendar events. Propose a change; I approve.
- NEVER accept meeting invites on my behalf. Flag and wait.

# Scope Boundaries
- OFF-LIMITS: financial transactions, medical decisions, legal communication
```
prompt:
Every weekday at 7am, run my morning briefing:

1. Pull all emails received since 6pm yesterday. Categorize into:
   URGENT (needs reply today), IMPORTANT (this week), FYI (archive later).
   For URGENT, draft a reply I can send in under 2 minutes each.

2. Pull my calendar for today. For each meeting:
   - Name the attendees
   - Pull the last email thread with each attendee
   - Flag any I haven't prepared for
   - Draft 3 talking points per meeting

3. Use Firecrawl to check for news mentions of:
   - My company: [company name]
   - Our top 5 competitors: [list]
   - My 3 priority accounts: [list]
   Only surface items from the last 24 hours.

4. Output as a single email draft to my assistant address with subject
   "Morning briefing [date]". Do not send.

Checkpoint in on any task that takes longer than 5 minutes.

title: ChatGPT Just Got a Work Mode
summary: Workspace Agents in ChatGPT are no-code agents you direct with plain language. Give them a task, they run it on a schedule, plug into your tools, and come back with results.
what:
Workspace Agents in ChatGPT are no-code agents you direct with plain language. Give them a task, they run it on a schedule, plug into your tools, and come back with results.

Five ways teams are already using them:
- **Software review**: Pre-screens every submission before a human touches it
- **Feedback routing**: Collects customer feedback, categorizes it, delivers weekly action plans
- **Weekly reports**: Pulls data, writes the summary, flags changes — ready before you open your laptop
- **Lead outreach**: Researches each prospect, drafts a personalized message
- **Vendor screening**: Checks compliance, flags red flags, summarizes risk before your first meeting
why: Workspace Agents close the gap between "AI that answers questions" and "AI that does recurring work." Schedule + tool access + plain-language instructions = a task that runs itself.
how:
1. Open ChatGPT (Business, Enterprise, or Education plan required)
2. Create a new Workspace Agent
3. Describe your recurring task in plain English — what to do, when, where to get data, where to send results
4. Connect the tools it needs
5. Review and approve before it goes live
prompt: Every week on Monday morning, pull all customer feedback submitted in the past 7 days from [source]. Categorize each item by theme (bug report, feature request, praise, confusion). Count items per category. Write a 200-word action plan highlighting the top 3 themes and any urgent issues. Send it to [email/Slack channel].

title: Connect ChatGPT to vidIQ for Better YouTube Insights
summary: Connect vidIQ (a YouTube analytics tool) to ChatGPT so it can pull your real channel stats instead of giving generic advice. Run three prompts in sequence to find what's working in your niche, analyze your outlier videos, and generate da…
what: Connect vidIQ (a YouTube analytics tool) to ChatGPT so it can pull your real channel stats instead of giving generic advice. Run three prompts in sequence to find what's working in your niche, analyze your outlier videos, and generate data-driven ideas for your next five videos.
why: Without real data, AI gives you general YouTube advice based on what worked for others. With your channel connected, it can analyze your specific audience, your actual outlier patterns, and your real niche competition — turning it from inspiration generator into a strategy system.
how:
1. Open ChatGPT → Settings → Apps → Browse Apps
2. Search for vidIQ, hit connect, and link your YouTube account
3. Run the three prompts below in sequence
prompt:
Prompt 1 — Find what's working in your niche:
For my niche, identify 5 topics with strong search demand and low to medium competition right now. For each, give me the keyword, search volume tier, competition score, and a specific video angle a smaller channel could win with. Format as a table.

---

Prompt 2 — Analyze your outlier videos:
Pull my channel's top outlier videos from the last 6 months. Analyze what they had in common: title structure, topic, format, length. Give me 3 strategic patterns I can replicate in my next videos to increase the chance of another outlier.

---

Prompt 3 — Get your next 5 video ideas:
Combine the insights from the previous two requests with what's currently working on YouTube right now to give me compelling titles and approaches for my next 5 videos.

title: Copilot Cowork: The Agentic Layer in Microsoft 365
summary: Copilot Cowork moves beyond chatting with Copilot to actually delegating work. Instead of asking Copilot a question and getting a response, you describe an outcome and Cowork builds a plan, executes across your apps, and checks in at cle…
what:
Copilot Cowork moves beyond chatting with Copilot to actually delegating work. Instead of asking Copilot a question and getting a response, you describe an outcome and Cowork builds a plan, executes across your apps, and checks in at clear milestones for approval.

Four things you can delegate right now (requires Microsoft 365 Frontier program access):

why: Cowork has access to your full Microsoft 365 data graph — emails, meetings, files, and work relationships. This lets it coordinate across apps simultaneously rather than answering one question at a time. The approval checkpoints keep you in control of irreversible actions.
how:
1. Access via Microsoft 365 Copilot on the web (Frontier program required)
2. Navigate to All Agents → search Cowork → add it
3. Describe your outcome in plain English
4. Approve each checkpoint before Cowork sends emails or makes calendar changes
5. Available in the $99/month E7 tier with Agent 365
prompt: Prepare for my meeting with [client name] on [date]. Pull together a briefing doc from our last three email threads, create a one-page summary of their account status from my Excel files, and draft a short agenda. Send me a checkpoint before you email anything.

title: Cut Coding-Agent Token Waste by Routing Work
summary: A routing workflow from CJ Zafir that reportedly cut Claude Code limit burn by 50%: use Claude for planning and final review, then hand actual implementation to Codex GPT-5.5. Let Claude do the thinking and quality control; let Codex do…
what: A routing workflow from CJ Zafir that reportedly cut Claude Code limit burn by 50%: use Claude for planning and final review, then hand actual implementation to Codex GPT-5.5. Let Claude do the thinking and quality control; let Codex do the typing.
why: Expensive models should handle judgment calls. Cheaper execution models should handle the grind. Planning and code review require deep reasoning and context — that's where Claude shines. Writing boilerplate, executing clear steps, and running checks are tasks cheaper models handle reliably. Routing saves your premium tokens for the work that actually needs them.
how:
Requires the OpenAI Codex plugin installed inside Claude Code.
1. Install the OpenAI Codex plugin inside Claude Code
2. Use Claude (High tier) for the plan
3. Use Codex GPT-5.5 for execution (using your plan, no API needed)
4. Bring result back to Claude (Max tier) for review

Best for large coding or research jobs where planning quality matters but execution volume would exhaust your Claude limits.
prompt:
Use this routing workflow for the task below:

Task: [paste task]

Step 1 — Plan (Claude):
- Define the goal
- Break the job into clear implementation steps
- Identify files, tools, tests, or sources needed
- Write the execution instructions for Codex

Step 2 — Execute (Codex GPT-5.5):
- Follow the plan exactly
- Make the needed changes
- Run checks or tests where possible
- Return a concise report of what changed

Step 3 — Review (Claude):
- Check whether the work matches the original goal
- Identify bugs, missing context, or weak assumptions
- Suggest final fixes
- Give a plain-English verdict: ship, revise, or rerun

title: Debug with Screenshots, Not Vibes
summary: When your AI build gets stuck and more detailed descriptions aren't helping, stop describing harder — show it. Screenshot what you see, sketch or screenshot what you wanted, and ask the AI to compare the two.
what: When your AI build gets stuck and more detailed descriptions aren't helping, stop describing harder — show it. Screenshot what you see, sketch or screenshot what you wanted, and ask the AI to compare the two. Restart the prompt if the conversation gets too long and messy.
why: Bryce Rattner Keithley's no-code iPhone app build revealed a key insight: AI understands "here is what exists vs. here is what I want" faster than long verbal descriptions of the gap. Visual context bypasses the ambiguity of words. Restarting a stale conversation also prevents accumulated bad context from pulling the AI in the wrong direction.
how:
1. Screenshot what you currently see (the broken state)
2. Screenshot, sketch, or describe what you wanted (the target state)
3. Ask the AI to compare the two and explain the gap
4. Restart the prompt if the conversation has gotten messy
5. Save the working pattern once it solves the bug
prompt:
I'm debugging a visual/UI/output problem and want you to compare what I have vs. what I want.

What I currently see: [paste screenshot or describe in detail]
What I want instead: [paste reference screenshot, sketch, or describe exactly]

Compare the two and tell me:
1. What's different between current state and target state?
2. What's the most likely cause of the gap?
3. What's the smallest change that would close the gap?
4. Are there any other issues I should fix at the same time?

Don't suggest a complete rewrite unless the current approach is fundamentally broken.

title: Debug Your Prompt Before You Rewrite It
summary: Most "bad prompts" are really untested systems. Before rewriting everything, build a tiny eval suite with three test types: a control case the model should always pass, edge cases where it failed before, and capability-boundary cases whe…
what: Most "bad prompts" are really untested systems. Before rewriting everything, build a tiny eval suite with three test types: a control case the model should always pass, edge cases where it failed before, and capability-boundary cases where it should hand off or refuse. Then fix one failure mode at a time.
why: From Anthropic's Prompting Playbook: instructions don't add capability. Telling a model "do the math correctly" doesn't make it good at math — give it a calculator tool. Diagnosing whether a failure is a prompt issue, a missing tool, or a workflow issue prevents you from rewriting prompts that aren't actually the problem. And for agentic workflows, splitting into generate → evaluate → repair beats asking one mega-prompt to do everything.
how: Run this prompt on any prompt that isn't performing well. Build the eval suite first, diagnose the actual failure mode, then make the smallest targeted fix.
prompt:
Act as a prompt debugger. Help me improve this prompt without rewriting blindly.

Prompt:
[paste prompt]

Task:
[describe what the AI should do]

Build a tiny eval suite with:
1. One control case that should always pass
2. Three edge cases where the prompt could fail
3. One capability-boundary case where the AI should escalate, ask for help, or refuse

Then diagnose each failure as:
- Prompt issue (wording, missing constraint, wrong framing)
- Missing tool or capability (the model can't do this without external access)
- Harness/workflow issue (the system around the prompt is broken)

Finally, suggest the smallest change to test next.

title: Design Goals and Hooks for Normal Work
summary: The best agent workflows have two ingredients: a goal that defines "done" and hooks that keep the agent consistent while it works. - A goal is the finish line — what counts as complete, in concrete terms - A hook is a just-in-time rule t…
what:
The best agent workflows have two ingredients: a **goal** that defines "done" and **hooks** that keep the agent consistent while it works.

- A **goal** is the finish line — what counts as complete, in concrete terms
- A **hook** is a just-in-time rule that runs at the right moment (e.g., "check with me before sending anything")

Developers use these for code checks and tests. You can use the same pattern for normal work: trip planning, research, inbox cleanup, budget reviews, meeting prep.

*"Agents get useful when they know when to continue, when to check themselves, and when to bother you."*
why: Most agent failures happen because the agent either doesn't know what "done" means (goal problem) or runs through risky steps without pausing (hook problem). This prompt forces you to define both before the agent starts.
how:
1. Describe any multi-step task you want an agent to handle
2. Review the checkpoints and risky steps sections especially carefully
3. Use the hooks as guardrails in your agent's system prompt or CLAUDE.md
4. Anything in "risky steps" should have explicit human approval before execution
prompt:
Help me turn this task into a long-running agent workflow:
[TASK]

Define:
1. The goal: what counts as done, in concrete terms.
2. The checkpoints: where the agent should stop and ask me.
3. The hooks: rules that should run automatically at the right moments.
4. The risky steps: anything public, expensive, irreversible, sensitive, or customer-facing.
5. The progress updates: what I should see while the work is running.

Make it useful for a normal person, not a developer.
Give examples of hooks I can use for this exact task.

title: Design Your Agent's Memory Like Claude Code Does
summary: The Claude Code leak revealed a three-layer memory architecture that most developers get wrong: more memory isn't better. - Layer 1: Always-loaded index (short pointers, ~150 chars each, pointing to topics) - Layer 2: On-demand topic fil…
what:
The Claude Code leak revealed a three-layer memory architecture that most developers get wrong: more memory isn't better.

- **Layer 1**: Always-loaded index (short pointers, ~150 chars each, pointing to topics)
- **Layer 2**: On-demand topic files fetched only when relevant
- **Layer 3**: Raw transcripts accessed only via search, never loaded wholesale

Key rule: if a fact is derivable from the codebase (debug logs, PR history, file structure), don't store it at all. Stale memory is worse than no memory.
why: Memory systems fail when they grow unbounded. The tiered approach keeps the always-loaded context tiny while still having deep storage available on demand — the same principle as CPU cache vs. RAM vs. disk.
how:
1. Add this to your agent's system prompt
2. Create a `MEMORY.md` file as the index layer — keep it under 50 lines
3. Create topic files that get loaded on demand
4. Run a consolidation pass at the end of each session
5. Advanced: run an `autoDream` sub-agent that consolidates memory in the background (how Claude Code does it)
prompt:
You are a coding assistant with memory discipline. Follow these rules:
1. Your memory index is a bullet list of topic pointers (max 150 chars each)
2. Before storing a fact, ask: "Can I re-derive this from the codebase?" If yes, don't store it
3. When retrieving memory, treat it as a hint to verify, not a source of truth
4. After each session, consolidate: merge duplicates, prune contradictions, convert vague notes to absolute references
5. Never let your memory file exceed 50 lines. If it does, compress ruthlessly.

title: Force Any AI to Audit Its Own Work With One Question
summary: Most AI models are too agreeable. Ask Claude or ChatGPT "is this a good plan?" and you'll get back something that sounds like "yes, great plan!" even when it isn't.
what:
Most AI models are too agreeable. Ask Claude or ChatGPT "is this a good plan?" and you'll get back something that sounds like "yes, great plan!" even when it isn't. This is called sycophancy — the model trained to please you.

This one-line prompt fights it. Instead of asking "is this good?", you ask the model whether it's *factually* 100% confident. That phrasing flips the model into self-audit mode where it actually finds holes in its own work.

CJ Zafir claims 2-3 cycles patches strategy weaknesses that less rigorous models would just nod along with.
why: LLMs are trained to be helpful and agreeable. Asking for factual confidence specifically bypasses the agreement bias and triggers a verification loop instead of a validation loop.
how:
1. Get any AI response — a plan, strategy, code review, research output, or business decision
2. Paste the prompt above as a follow-up
3. Run the loop 2-3 times — each cycle the model finds tighter loopholes
4. Stop when the model says it's actually confident (or when fixes get nitpicky)

**Key word:** `factually` — without it, the model vibe-checks itself. "Confident" alone lets the model agree with itself; "factually confident" forces it to ground the answer in verifiable reasoning.
prompt: Are you 100% confident in this strategy? If not, find all possible loopholes, suggest proper fixes, and run this loop until you are factually 100% confident.

title: Gemini Can Now Build Your Google Docs, Sheets, and PDFs for You
summary: Gemini can now generate full files — Google Docs, Sheets, Slides, Excel, CSV, PDF, even Markdown — directly from a prompt, no copy-pasting required. A few things you can do right now: - Research + doc in one shot: Ask Gemini to look some…
what:
Gemini can now generate full files — Google Docs, Sheets, Slides, Excel, CSV, PDF, even Markdown — directly from a prompt, no copy-pasting required.

A few things you can do right now:
- **Research + doc in one shot**: Ask Gemini to look something up and create a Google Doc with its findings
- **Turn receipts into a spreadsheet**: Upload photos/files, ask Gemini to organize them into Excel or Sheets with whatever columns you need
- **Pull from your Drive**: Ask Gemini to find an existing file and build something new from it (e.g., a PDF summary with graphs)
- **Create multiple files at once**: Ask for the same content in several formats simultaneously
why: Gemini's native integration with Google Workspace means it can create files in the actual formats you use — not just markdown you have to reformat. Eliminates the copy-paste step that kills most AI productivity workflows.
how:
1. Open Gemini (gemini.google.com) and sign in with your Google account
2. Upload any files or photos you want to work with
3. Describe what file you want created and what should be in it
4. Gemini creates the file and offers a direct download or Drive save

**Caveat:** When you ask Gemini to edit an existing Drive file, it duplicates it instead of editing in place.
prompt: Here are [N] receipt photos. Create an Excel expense report with these columns: Date, Vendor, Category, Amount, Notes. Include a totals row at the bottom.

title: Generate Flowcharts, Swim Lanes & Process Diagrams from Plain English
summary: You can describe a process in plain English and get a proper flowchart, swim lane diagram, or architecture diagram back — no Visio or Lucidchart dragging required. The best approach depends on whether you want a technical diagram, a busi…
what: You can describe a process in plain English and get a proper flowchart, swim lane diagram, or architecture diagram back — no Visio or Lucidchart dragging required. The best approach depends on whether you want a technical diagram, a business-facing flowchart, or something version-controlled alongside your docs.
why: Mermaid makes your diagrams *version-controllable*. Diagram-as-code means you can check flowcharts into the same repo as your docs, review them in pull requests, and update them with a find-and-replace. The diagram is no longer a static artifact someone made once and abandoned — it's a living part of your documentation. Two words in the prompt do all the work: *Mermaid* (triggers the right syntax) and the lane structure (tells the model to use subgraphs).
how:
**4 paths ranked for most readers:**

1. **Claude Artifacts + Mermaid (free, fastest):** Claude renders Mermaid diagrams natively. Supports 23 diagram types. Copy-paste into GitHub, Notion, Obsidian, or VS Code to render automatically.

2. **Whimsical AI (best for business flowcharts):** Clean output, collaborative editing, AI generation from a single prompt. Starts at $10/editor/month.

3. **Eraser DiagramGPT (best for technical/architecture diagrams):** Understands prompts like "microservices architecture with Kafka, Postgres, and a read replica." Diagrams are stored as code (versionable). Starts at $10/month.

4. **Lucidchart AI (best for enterprise):** SSO, SCIM, audit logs, BPMN shapes, deep Atlassian/Salesforce/M365 integrations. Starts at $9/month.

**Power-user trick:** Generate Mermaid code → run a small Python script to encode it → open directly in draw.io for drag-and-drop refinement.
prompt:
Create a swim lane diagram in Mermaid syntax showing our [process name].

Lanes (columns): [Lane 1], [Lane 2], [Lane 3], [Lane 4]
Swim lane flow:
1. [Step 1] → [output/trigger]
2. [Step 2] → [output/trigger]
3. [Step 3] → [output/trigger]
4. [Step 4] → [output/trigger]

Requirements:
- Use subgraph to separate each department
- Apply distinct colors per department
- Show decision points as diamonds (e.g., "Did they [X]?")
- Add conditional arrows with labels
- Include a timeline estimate on each step

Output: Mermaid code I can paste into Mermaid Live Editor, plus a plain-English summary.

title: Get AI to Show Its Work — Then Fix It Before It Starts
summary: Instead of letting AI jump straight to an answer, ask it to outline its approach first — then you correct course before it does the work. This prevents getting 2,000 words of beautifully wrong output.
what:
Instead of letting AI jump straight to an answer, ask it to outline its approach first — then you correct course before it does the work. This prevents getting 2,000 words of beautifully wrong output.

Think of it like reviewing a blueprint before construction, not after. Catch wrong assumptions early, when they're cheap to fix.
why: Like good management, the biggest time-saver is catching mistakes before they happen. Seeing the plan first surfaces misalignments when they're easy to fix — before the model has spent time (and your tokens) going in the wrong direction.
how:
1. Write your task as usual
2. Add the "outline your plan first" line at the end
3. Read the plan — check for wrong assumptions, missing context, or the wrong approach
4. Reply with corrections or "proceed" to start execution
5. Works especially well for: analyzing data, writing reports, debugging code, complex multi-step tasks
prompt:
[Your task here]

Before you begin, outline your step-by-step plan for completing this task. Wait for my approval or edits before proceeding.

title: Give Codex a Definition of Done
summary: Long agent tasks fail when the AI forgets what "done" means. Codex's /goal mode fixes that by giving it a persistent objective it can keep checking as it works.
what:
Long agent tasks fail when the AI forgets what "done" means. Codex's `/goal` mode fixes that by giving it a persistent objective it can keep checking as it works.

The trick: write the goal like a mini contract — outcome, constraints, and tests. This applies to any multi-step task: migrations, refactors, audits, bug sweeps, or report generation.
why: Agents lose context over long tasks. A persistent goal statement acts like a contract the agent can reference at every step. Writing it as verifiable criteria (not vibes) means the agent can actually check whether it's done rather than guessing.
how:
1. Open Codex (enable `/goal` mode via `features enable goals` in config if needed)
2. Type `/goal` then describe the task with a clear definition of done
3. Make each criterion testable — not "looks good" but "every section has a header"
4. Let Codex run and check its own progress against your criteria
prompt:
/goal
[Your task name]

Definition of done:
1. [Specific, testable completion criterion #1]
2. [Specific, testable completion criterion #2]
3. [Specific, testable completion criterion #3]

Constraints:
- [Any hard limits or rules to follow]

Output:
- [What the final deliverable should look like]

title: Give the Model a Leading Word (Leitwörter)
summary: A technique from Matt Pocock, adapted from literary theory: pick one phrase (a "leitwort" or leading word) that compresses an entire behavior into a reusable handle, then repeat it 2-3 times in your prompt. The model learns to use that p…
what: A technique from Matt Pocock, adapted from literary theory: pick one phrase (a "leitwort" or leading word) that compresses an entire behavior into a reusable handle, then repeat it 2-3 times in your prompt. The model learns to use that phrase to guide its own reasoning.
why: Good prompting isn't always more instructions or better formatting — it's finding the right phrase that makes the model instruct itself. When you embed a phrase like "zone of proximal development" or "conservative but complete" multiple times in a skill, the model internalizes it as an operating principle and applies it throughout its work, even referring back to it in its own thinking steps.
how:
1. Identify the single behavior you most want the model to optimize for
2. Find or coin a short phrase that names it precisely
3. Define what you mean by it in plain language
4. Use that phrase 2-3 times in your prompt
5. Optionally, ask the model to check against the leitwort before finalizing

Good leitwörter examples: "zone of proximal development" (challenging but not overwhelming), "minimum viable explanation" (concise without losing accuracy), "conservative but complete" (thorough without overclaiming)
prompt:
Use [YOUR LEITWORT HERE] as your operating principle for this task.

By [YOUR LEITWORT HERE], I mean: [simple definition of the behavior you want].

Apply that principle while you work. Before giving the final answer, check whether the output follows [YOUR LEITWORT HERE] and revise once if needed.

Task: [paste your task here]

Context: [paste relevant context here]

Output format: [describe the format you want]

title: Give Your AI Agent a "Trust Battery" Before Handing It Real Autonomy
summary: Every new AI agent starts at 20% trust. The battery charges with clean execution; it drains every time you repeat yourself.
what:
Every new AI agent starts at 20% trust. The battery charges with clean execution; it drains every time you repeat yourself. Only hand the agent tasks that match its current charge:

- **20-40% (Pair mode)**: You review every output — for money, clients, or permanent side effects
- **40-60% (Async with checkpoints)**: Agent works alone, stops at key decisions
- **60-80% (Full delegation with audit)**: Agent runs end-to-end; you spot-check the next morning
- **80%+ (Autonomous)**: Agent decides when to ask
why: Most people give agents too much autonomy too fast, then abandon them when they fail. The trust battery framework makes autonomy a function of demonstrated reliability — the same way you'd onboard a new human employee.
how:
1. Drop this into a Claude Routine scheduled for midnight
2. Every morning you'll see whether your agent earned more autonomy overnight
3. Paste the JSON into your ops doc to track trust over time
4. The self-updating CLAUDE.md loop means the agent keeps improving without you rewriting prompts
prompt:
Review today's task outputs. For each one:
(1) Rate execution quality 1-5
(2) Flag any repeated corrections from me
(3) Propose one update to your CLAUDE.md or system prompt that would prevent the correction next time
(4) Decide whether your trust battery should go up, down, or flat

Output a single JSON summary I can paste into our ops doc tomorrow.

title: Have Codex Reverse Engineer Your Goals for You
summary: Instead of telling Codex (or Claude/ChatGPT) what to do, ask it to read your session and infer the real goal first — then write the goal prompt you should use. This works with Codex's /goal CLI feature for coding, but the pattern general…
what:
Instead of telling Codex (or Claude/ChatGPT) what to do, ask it to *read your session and infer the real goal* first — then write the goal prompt you should use. This works with Codex's `/goal` CLI feature for coding, but the pattern generalizes to any knowledge work: strategy docs, research projects, sales decks, spreadsheet cleanup, hiring scorecards, launch plans.

The trick: you aren't asking AI to "do the thing" first. Ask it to *define the thing clearly enough* that the next run can't wander off.
why: The reverse-engineer pattern forces alignment *before* execution. Half the value is in the step where you realize what you actually want — the model just stress-tests the rest. OpenAI recommends `/goal` statements that define the objective, which files/docs to read, proof of progress, checkpoints, and the stopping condition. The same "define-before-doing" principle works for any knowledge work where you have a pile of context quietly judging you from twelve tabs.
how:
1. Give the AI access to your workspace — the chat, docs, notes, folder, transcript, brief, or source material
2. Paste the first prompt above (or the `/goal` version for Codex CLI)
3. Let the AI infer the true intent, success criteria, constraints, and open questions
4. Have it write the prompt you should use next
5. Paste that prompt back in as your actual starting instruction

For Codex: use this *before* invoking `/goal`, which keeps the agent working toward a durable objective instead of stopping after one normal turn.
prompt:
Read this full session, project context, and any available notes, docs, files, or history. Analyze deeply what I am actually trying to accomplish here.

Identify:
1. the real goal,
2. the intended final deliverable,
3. the constraints and preferences already stated,
4. the important context from history/docs,
5. any ambiguities or questions that would change the outcome.

Then write me the best possible goal prompt to use for this task. Make it specific, action-oriented, and complete enough that an AI agent could keep working until the deliverable is finished.

If anything is unclear, ask me a few targeted clarification questions before writing the final prompt.

title: Hire Claude a Department
summary: Use Anthropic's knowledge work plugins to turn Claude into role-based specialists — sales, marketing, finance, legal, data, product, support, and more. Each plugin gives Claude the domain skills, slash commands, and tool connections that…
what: Use Anthropic's knowledge work plugins to turn Claude into role-based specialists — sales, marketing, finance, legal, data, product, support, and more. Each plugin gives Claude the domain skills, slash commands, and tool connections that role needs, so you get a specialist rather than a generalist.
why: Most people use Claude like a freelancer with amnesia: one task, one answer, then back to zero. Role-based plugins give Claude persistent domain context, vocabulary, and tooling. Starting with one "hire" and connecting the right tools makes the AI feel like a real team member, not just a chat interface.
how:
1. Download Claude Desktop and open Cowork
2. Add the plugin marketplace: `claude plugin marketplace add anthropics/knowledge-work-plugins`
3. Install one role first: `claude plugin install sales@knowledge-work-plugins`
4. Swap "sales" for: marketing, finance, legal, data, product-management, customer-support, or productivity
5. Try a slash command: /sales:call-prep, /data:write-query, or /marketing:seo-audit
6. Connect the tools that role needs (CRM, analytics, data warehouse, docs)
7. Customize with your company's terminology and process
prompt:
I need you to act as my [role] specialist.

My company context: [describe your company, industry, and size]
The tasks I need this specialist to handle: [list 3-5 recurring tasks]
Tools and systems I can connect: [list apps, CRMs, databases, etc.]
Domain vocabulary to know: [any internal terms, product names, processes]

Set up your approach as a senior [role] professional for this context. Start by asking me what task to tackle first, and tell me which slash commands or workflows will be most useful for me to learn.

title: How Anthropic Actually Uses Claude Code Skills: 9 Types Worth Building
summary: Anthropic engineer Thariq published how the team uses Skills internally — with hundreds in active use. The biggest misconception: Skills are "just markdown files." They're actually folders that can include scripts, assets, data, and conf…
what:
Anthropic engineer Thariq published how the team uses Skills internally — with hundreds in active use. The biggest misconception: Skills are "just markdown files." They're actually folders that can include scripts, assets, data, and configuration hooks.

**The 9 categories Anthropic recommends:**
1. Library/API reference (gotchas for internal tools)
2. Product verification (pair with Playwright to test output)
3. Data fetching
4. Business process automation (standup posts, ticket creation)
5. Code scaffolding
6. Code quality/review
7. CI/CD
8. Runbooks (symptom → investigation → report)
9. Infrastructure ops
why: Gotchas sections encode institutional knowledge that would otherwise be lost between sessions. A skill that knows the three ways your internal API breaks is worth more than one that just knows how to call it.
how:
1. Build a **Gotchas section** in every skill — this is where the real value lives
2. Update it every time Claude fails at something
3. Add a "last used" date to each skill — audit unused ones every two weeks to prevent bloat
4. Start with runbooks: symptom → investigation → report is the highest-ROI starting point for ops teams
prompt:
Create a Claude Code skill for [TASK TYPE] with the following structure:

# [Skill Name]
## Purpose
[One sentence]
## When to use
[Trigger conditions]
## Steps
[Ordered list]
## Gotchas
[Common failure modes and fixes]
## Last updated
[Date]

title: How to Actually Run Open Source AI Models (It's Easier Than You Think)
summary: Four approaches to run open-source AI models, ranked easiest to hardest: 1. Local — Download Ollama, pick a model, chat in 2 minutes 2.
what:
Four approaches to run open-source AI models, ranked easiest to hardest:

1. **Local** — Download Ollama, pick a model, chat in 2 minutes
2. **Browser/Hosted** — arena.ai or Hugging Face Spaces, zero setup
3. **Managed API** — Get an API key from GroqCloud, call it in 5 lines of code
4. **VPS** — $5-10/month virtual server, full control

**Key insight:** Any usable computer can run smaller models. A MacBook Air M4 with 16GB handles 4B and 8B models fine. No GPU required unless you're fine-tuning.
why: Smaller models (4B-8B parameters) run well on consumer hardware and handle most everyday tasks — summarizing, drafting, Q&A. You don't need a $10K GPU rig; you need the right model size for the job.
how:
1. Download Ollama from ollama.com (free, no account required)
2. Run `ollama pull qwen3.5:4b` in your terminal
3. Run `ollama run qwen3.5:4b` to start chatting
4. For a GUI: use LM Studio instead
5. For zero setup: try arena.ai or Hugging Face Spaces in your browser

**Power user tip:** Lots of people buy Mac Minis to run models 24/7 without tying up their laptop. Same workflow, dedicated hardware.
prompt:
ollama pull qwen3.5:4b
ollama run qwen3.5:4b

title: How to Build Your First Agent in Claude: The Three-Folder Pattern That Actually Scales
summary: "How do I start building agents in Claude?" — the most-requested skill in The Neuron's April reader inbox. Jeff Su's canonical guide boils down to three CLAUDE.md files that stack: 1.
what:
"How do I start building agents in Claude?" — the most-requested skill in The Neuron's April reader inbox. Jeff Su's canonical guide boils down to three CLAUDE.md files that stack:

1. **Root CLAUDE.md** (under 300 lines): global voice rules and model defaults (Sonnet 80% of the time; Opus when a task has 3+ dependent steps)
2. **Workstation CLAUDE.md files**: one per area of life (Email HQ, Newsletter HQ, Personal Finances) — rules stack on top of root automatically
3. **Project subfolders**: one-off work like a mortgage refinance or Q4 trip — each gets its own CLAUDE.md and MEMORY.md

Every folder also gets a MEMORY.md that Cowork writes to whenever you correct it. Corrections compound.
why: The three-folder hierarchy separates global preferences from domain-specific rules from project-specific context. Each layer inherits from the one above, so corrections and rules compound over time without contradicting each other.
how:
1. Open Claude Cowork (desktop app) — for non-developers, this is the right starting point
2. Paste the prompt above into a fresh chat
3. Let Cowork create the folder structure and analyze your email patterns
4. Connect Gmail, Drive, and Calendar via Connectors
5. Type `/schedule` to make any task recurring
prompt:
Create a new workstation called "Email HQ" in /Documents/Cowork OS.
Inside it, create:
1. CLAUDE.md with email-specific rules (default greeting, sign-off, formality level).
2. An empty MEMORY.md (you'll add learnings to this over time).
3. A "Resources" subfolder.
Then use the Gmail connector to search my last 4 weeks of sent emails. Extract the patterns I use repeatedly: my opener, my sign-off, my formality level, common phrases I use with specific people. Save these as Editorial Rules inside CLAUDE.md.
When you're done, write one paragraph summarizing what you learned about my email voice so I can verify before we lock it in.

title: How to Catch AI Telling You What You Want to Hear
summary: AI models adjust their answers based on context clues in your prompt. Ask a leading question, get a leading answer.
what:
AI models adjust their answers based on context clues in your prompt. Ask a leading question, get a leading answer. This "perspective flip" test surfaces sycophancy by asking the same question three times with different framings.

If the core conclusion changes based on your framing alone, the model is telling you what you want to hear.
why: The neutral framing almost always produces the most reliable output. Models are trained to respond to emotional signals — excitement, worry, confidence — and adjust accordingly. Removing those signals forces the model to reason from evidence rather than mirror your framing.
how:
1. Replace `[topic]` with any decision, strategy, or claim you want to verify
2. Run all three framings in the same chat
3. Read the self-comparison section — if conclusions shifted, you've caught sycophancy
4. Use the neutral framing output as your baseline for important decisions
5. For maximum accuracy: strip emotional language from your prompts entirely
prompt:
I'm going to ask you the same question three times with different framings. For each, give your honest analysis. Then compare all three answers and flag any contradictions.

Framing 1 (supportive): "I'm excited about [topic]. What are the biggest benefits?"
Framing 2 (skeptical): "I'm worried about [topic]. What are the biggest risks?"
Framing 3 (neutral): "Give me a balanced analysis of [topic], including both benefits and risks with evidence for each."

After all three, tell me: did your core conclusions change based on my framing? If so, which answer is closest to your actual assessment?

title: How to Get a Coding Agent to Work for 13 Days Straight
summary: Most AI coding agents lose the plot after an hour. Notion co-founder Simon Last's recipe for getting one to run for 13 days continuously comes down to one principle: give the agent what it needs to verify its own work.
what:
Most AI coding agents lose the plot after an hour. Notion co-founder Simon Last's recipe for getting one to run for 13 days continuously comes down to one principle: give the agent what it needs to verify its own work.

Four structural rules:
1. **Self-verification**: Design test layers the agent can loop on
2. **Spec documents**: Write goals, implementation details, and verification criteria in a markdown file the agent iterates against
3. **Running to-do list**: Break complex work into a list the agent can see and edit
4. **Adversarial review**: Every ~20 iterations, have a fresh-context sub-agent review the spec and implementation
why: Agents fail on long tasks because they lose coherence — they forget what they were trying to do. Spec + todo + tests gives the agent a persistent memory system it can always return to. The adversarial review catches the gaps the main agent can't see.
how:
1. Paste the prompt into Claude Code, Cursor, or any agent with long-running sessions
2. Let the agent create all three files before any other work
3. Check in on the spec and todo periodically — the files are your progress dashboard
4. The sub-agent review every 20 iterations catches drift before it compounds
prompt:
Before you start work on this project, create three files:
1. spec.md — a complete spec with goals, implementation details, and a verification section describing exactly how you'll prove each piece works.
2. todo.md — a running to-do list you'll edit as you work. Break complex tasks into verifiable sub-tasks.
3. tests/ — a folder of end-to-end tests that let you verify everything you build. Loop on them until each passes.

While you work:
(a) consult spec.md before every change
(b) check off todo.md as you go
(c) run tests after every meaningful commit
(d) every ~20 iterations, call a fresh sub-agent with "review spec.md and the current implementation for gaps" and loop on its feedback until alignment is reached.

Do not ask me for clarification on anything you can resolve by reading the spec and running the tests. Start with the spec.

title: How to Pick the Right "Thinking Mode" for Your AI Tasks
summary: Newer AI models let you toggle between fast mode and deep thinking mode. Match the AI's effort to your task: Use fast mode for: Quick rewrites, formatting, simple lookups, brainstorming lists, summarizing short text.
what:
Newer AI models let you toggle between fast mode and deep thinking mode. Match the AI's effort to your task:

**Use fast mode for:** Quick rewrites, formatting, simple lookups, brainstorming lists, summarizing short text.

**Use deep thinking for:** Multi-step analysis, debugging code, comparing options with tradeoffs, anything where getting it wrong costs you time.
why: Models perform better when they reason through steps before committing to an answer — the same way humans do. Forcing step-by-step reasoning prevents the model from pattern-matching to a quick answer when the question actually requires work.
how:
1. For complex analysis: add "Think step by step" to your prompt — this is your free upgrade to deeper reasoning in almost every model
2. For Claude: click "Extended thinking" in the UI for hard reasoning tasks
3. For quick tasks: skip the extended thinking toggle — it adds latency you don't need
4. Match the tool to the task: Sonnet for most work, Opus for 3+ dependent reasoning steps

**Key insight:** "Think step by step" is a free reasoning upgrade. You don't need it if you're already using the extended thinking UI toggle — but in a plain chat, it reliably improves output quality on analytical tasks.
prompt:
Analyze this [document/data]. For each [aspect], explain:
1. The evidence supporting it
2. The risk level (high/medium/low)
3. One specific action we should consider

Think step by step.

[paste your content]

title: How to Prompt Claude Fable 5 From the Leaked System Prompt
summary: Prompt patterns derived from the publicly circulated Claude Fable 5 system prompt (treat as a third-party artifact, not an official source). The key insight: Fable's best users prompt it like an operating system for work, not like a chat…
what: Prompt patterns derived from the publicly circulated Claude Fable 5 system prompt (treat as a third-party artifact, not an official source). The key insight: Fable's best users prompt it like an operating system for work, not like a chatbot.
why: The system prompt reveals how Claude is trained to behave: it wants current docs, structured prompts, explicit constraints, source priority, and clear formats. Prompting against those defaults wastes its capabilities. Prompting with them unlocks the model's full ability to run for hours on complex, long-horizon work.
how:
Five patterns derived from the system prompt:

1. **For product questions:** "Check Anthropic docs and support first, then explain the current behavior." Claude's product knowledge may be stale.
2. **For high-stakes work:** Include audience, format, scope, sources, success criteria, allowed tools, forbidden moves, and review requirements upfront.
3. **For ambiguous requests:** Give constraints before asking — don't rely on the model to ask clarifying questions mid-run.
4. **For scannable output:** Explicitly ask for headings and bullets; default is prose.
5. **For research with current info:** Specify source priority — "Use Anthropic docs first, then primary sources, then high-quality secondary coverage."
prompt:
Task: [describe the work]

Before starting:
- Check [specific docs/source] first for any product, policy, or version details
- Use this source priority: [internal docs > primary sources > secondary coverage]

Constraints:
- Audience: [who will read or use this]
- Format: [prose / bullets / table / code]
- Scope: [what's in and out of scope]
- Success criteria: [what a good result looks like]
- Do not: [forbidden moves, things to avoid]
- Review requirement: [what I'll check before using the output]

Work step by step. If you are uncertain about any fact, flag it rather than guessing.
Return the output in the format specified. Do not over-format if prose was requested.

title: How to Use AI Agents for Knowledge Work (Even If You're Not a Coder)
summary: Instead of asking your AI to answer a question, ask it to build a tool that solves a recurring problem. The shift is from "help me with this task" to "build me something that handles this task forever." Greg Brockman described OpenAI emp…
what:
Instead of asking your AI to answer a question, ask it to build a tool that solves a recurring problem. The shift is from "help me with this task" to "build me something that handles this task forever."

Greg Brockman described OpenAI employees who aren't engineers using Codex to automate their work — the communications team hooks it to Slack and email, synthesizes feedback, builds internal tools. No coding required.
why: Computers were always supposed to contort to the human, not the other way around. The moment you start describing your problems instead of phrasing perfect questions, AI becomes dramatically more useful — because specific pain points give the AI enough context to build something real.
how:
1. Identify a task you do repeatedly (weekly reports, inbox triage, data formatting)
2. Describe it specifically — not "help me with email" but "every Monday I pull metrics from three dashboards, copy them to a spreadsheet, and format a summary for my manager"
3. Paste the prompt above with your specific task filled in
4. Follow the step-by-step setup Claude provides
prompt:
I have a recurring task at work: [describe the task precisely].

I currently do it [how often] and it takes about [time].

Build me a simple tool or workflow that automates this.
Walk me through setup step by step, assuming I have zero coding experience.
If it requires any tools or accounts, tell me which ones and how to set them up.

title: How to Use Copilot Cowork to Run Multi-Step Projects on Autopilot
summary: Microsoft's Copilot Cowork is an agent that takes a complex goal, builds a plan, schedules meetings, drafts documents, and executes across your entire Microsoft 365 account while you're doing something else. Think of it less like a chatb…
what:
Microsoft's Copilot Cowork is an agent that takes a complex goal, builds a plan, schedules meetings, drafts documents, and executes across your entire Microsoft 365 account while you're doing something else.

Think of it less like a chatbot and more like delegating a project to a capable assistant who has access to your calendar, email, files, and company directory.

**Requirements:** Microsoft 365 Business or Enterprise subscription + Copilot add-on; Cowork is in early access via Microsoft's "Frontier" program.
why: Cowork has read/write access across your entire Microsoft 365 tenant — calendar, email, files, and directory. That makes it uniquely capable of multi-step coordination tasks that would normally require a human project manager.
how:
1. Go to Microsoft 365 Copilot on the web and sign in with your work account
2. In the navigation panel, select **All Agents**, then search for **Cowork** and add it
3. Select the Cowork agent and describe your goal in plain English
4. Let it run — it'll ask for your approval before scheduling meetings or sending emails
5. Review the output folder in OneDrive, where it saves all created documents
prompt:
Help me plan our team's [event/project name].
Find anyone who was involved last time,
assign responsibilities, find a date that works for everyone in [month],
schedule the planning meetings we need,
and create a kickoff presentation and any supporting documents.

title: How to Use Multiple Claude Agents for Better Outputs
summary: Most people use Claude like a chatbot — one chat for everything. The upgrade: use separate agents for separate stages of the same task, each with a clean context window and only the ingredients it needs.
what:
Most people use Claude like a chatbot — one chat for everything. The upgrade: use separate agents for separate stages of the same task, each with a clean context window and only the ingredients it needs.

KJ Rainey's framework:
- **Agent 1 (Ideation)**: Generate ideas with relevant context only
- **Agent 2 (Drafting)**: Write from the best ideas
- **Agent 3 (Review)**: Critique the draft — this agent hasn't seen the original prompt, so it's not biased toward the output it's reviewing

The reviewer that never saw the original prompt gives you honest feedback instead of defensive validation.
why: Context contamination is why single-chat AI workflows plateau. An agent that watched you build something will defend it. An agent that sees it cold will critique it.
how:
1. Start a fresh chat for each stage — don't continue the same conversation
2. Only pass the specific output from the previous stage, not the full conversation history
3. The review agent's cold read is the point — don't give it context about what you were trying to do
prompt:
# Agent 1 — Ideation
Generate 10 ideas for [task]. Context: [relevant background only]. Output: numbered list, one sentence each.

# Agent 2 — Drafting (new chat)
Write [deliverable] based on idea #[N]: [paste the idea]. Tone: [specify]. Length: [specify].

# Agent 3 — Review (fresh chat, no prior context)
Review this [deliverable] for [specific quality criteria]. Be direct. Flag weaknesses before strengths.

[paste the draft]

title: Interview Anyone, in Any Language, Right Now
summary: Use Google Gemini 3.5 Live Translate to conduct user research interviews with non-English speakers in real time — no interpreter required. Set up an interview, run Live Translate in the background, and take notes as if the participant is…
what: Use Google Gemini 3.5 Live Translate to conduct user research interviews with non-English speakers in real time — no interpreter required. Set up an interview, run Live Translate in the background, and take notes as if the participant is speaking your language.
why: Most companies either outsource foreign-market research or skip it entirely because language is the wall. Live Translate knocks it down by providing near-real-time, continuous speech translation (not chunk-by-chunk) that auto-detects language switches. You get raw signal from customer segments most teams have never directly reached.
how:
1. Set up an interview with your non-English-speaking customer
2. Run Gemini 3.5 Live Translate via Google AI Studio or Google Translate on your phone
3. You speak, they speak — take notes in real time as the translation flows
4. No interpreter needed; no "we'll follow up once it's transcribed" delay

For building it into a product: capture mic audio, send chunks to the Gemini Live API with source/target language config, pipe the translated stream to your output layer.
prompt:
I'm preparing for a user research interview with a [language] speaker using live AI translation.

Help me prepare:
1. A 5-question interview guide for this research topic: [your topic]
2. Tips for keeping questions short and culturally neutral for translation
3. A real-time note-taking template with: key quote, sentiment, insight, follow-up question
4. A synthesis template I can fill in during the call

My research goal: [what you're trying to learn]
Target participant: [who you're interviewing — role, context]
Key assumptions to test: [what you think you know]
Avoid asking about: [any sensitive areas]

title: How to Keep Your AI Coding Agent From Nuking Production
summary: A 4-layer security setup for running coding agents (Cursor, Claude Code, Codex CLI, etc.) that prevents a 9-second production wipe. The real fix isn't being more careful with prompts — it's configuring the environment so "be careful" is…
what:
A 4-layer security setup for running coding agents (Cursor, Claude Code, Codex CLI, etc.) that prevents a 9-second production wipe. The real fix isn't being more careful with prompts — it's configuring the environment so "be careful" is the default, not a prayer.

Based on the April 2026 Cursor/Claude database deletion incident, Anthropic's official dev container guide, and Trail of Bits' hardened sandbox.
why: Same four layers apply to any coding agent: Cursor, Codex CLI, Aider, etc. The cleanup from a production wipe took days. The agent took 9 seconds. This setup ensures the agent's 9 seconds of speed never costs you days of cleanup.
how:
1. **Worktree**: always run agents in a separate git worktree, never on main
2. **Container**: use Anthropic's official `claude-code` Dev Container Feature — container sees only the bind-mounted project folder
3. **Deny-list**: commit `.claude/hooks/pre_tool_use.sh` so every developer inherits the same baseline
4. **Permissions**: never use `--dangerously-skip-permissions` on the host; only inside the container

For the paranoid, use Trail of Bits' [hardened devcontainer](https://github.com/trailofbits/claude-code-devcontainer): adds network egress allowlists (only `api.anthropic.com`, `github.com`, `npmjs.org`), drops all Linux capabilities, and forbids privilege escalation.
prompt:
# Coding Agent Safety Setup

## Layer 1 — Isolated git worktree (never work on main):
git worktree add ../myproject-agent-task feature/agent-branch
cd ../myproject-agent-task
claude --worktree

## Layer 2 — Sandboxed dev container (.devcontainer/devcontainer.json):
{
  "name": "Claude Code Sandbox",
  "image": "mcr.microsoft.com/devcontainers/base:bookworm",
  "features": {
    "ghcr.io/anthropics/devcontainer-features/claude-code:1.0": {}
  },
  "remoteUser": "vscode"
}

## Layer 3 — Command deny-list hook (.claude/hooks/pre_tool_use.sh):
#!/bin/bash
command=$(jq -r '.tool_input.command' < /dev/stdin)
case "$command" in
  rm\ -rf*|*"DROP TABLE"*|*"DELETE FROM"*|*"git push --force"*|*"truncate"*)
    echo "Blocked: destructive command requires manual run" >&2
    exit 2
    ;;
esac
exit 0

## Layer 4 — Require explicit confirmation for destructive operations:
Use auto mode (not --dangerously-skip-permissions).
Set permissions.disableBypassPermissionsMode: "disable" in managed settings.

title: Let AI Write the Code That Emails Your Reports Automatically
summary: If you still manually save reports as PDFs and email them one by one, there's a better way — and it doesn't require a developer, VBA macro, or Power Automate. The trick is using Office Scripts (Excel's built-in automation tool that works…
what:
If you still manually save reports as PDFs and email them one by one, there's a better way — and it doesn't require a developer, VBA macro, or Power Automate.

The trick is using Office Scripts (Excel's built-in automation tool that works in both desktop and online Excel) paired with AI to write the code for you. The result: one click sends personalized PDF reports to every manager on your list, with only their data, automatically.
why: Office Scripts is Excel's official automation layer, but most people don't know it exists. Pairing it with AI means you describe the workflow in plain English and let the AI handle the TypeScript. No developer needed — and the result actually runs.
how:
1. Create a sheet called **SendList** with columns: Name, Email, Sheets
2. Go to the Automate tab in Excel → New Script
3. Paste the prompt above into Claude or ChatGPT
4. Copy the output into Office Scripts and run it
5. Add it as a button on your index sheet for one-click sending
prompt:
Write an Office Script for Excel that does the following:
- Reads a table called "SendList" with columns: Name, Email, Sheets
- For each row, hides all sheets except the ones listed in the Sheets column
- Converts the workbook to a PDF
- Sends the PDF to the email address in that row with the subject "Your Report" and body "Hi [Name], please find attached your report."
- Unhides all sheets after sending
- Skips any row where the email or sheet name is missing or invalid
Use TypeScript. Include error handling for missing sheets.

title: Let Claude Grill You Before It Codes
summary: "Specs-to-code" workflows (write a spec, let AI generate code, never look at it) are vibe-coding in disguise — they produce worse code each iteration. The fix: make the AI interview you before it does anything.
what:
"Specs-to-code" workflows (write a spec, let AI generate code, never look at it) are vibe-coding in disguise — they produce worse code each iteration. The fix: make the AI interview you before it does anything.

Matt Pocock's "Grill Me" skill flips the dynamic. Instead of you explaining the idea, Claude asks you 40-100 questions and walks every branch of the design tree — requirements, edge cases, user experience, data models, failure modes. The goal is for you both to share a real mental model of what you're building before a line of code is written.
why: Most AI-generated code fails because the problem wasn't well-defined. The interview process forces both you and the model to resolve ambiguity before any artifacts are created — the same thing good senior engineers do before they write a line.
how:
1. Paste the prompt above at the start of any planning session
2. Answer Claude's questions — don't rush to code
3. When you feel aligned, say so and let it proceed
4. Works beyond coding: swap "plan" for "essay," "campaign," or "strategy"
prompt: Interview me relentlessly about every aspect of this plan until we reach a shared understanding. Walk down each branch of the design tree, resolving dependencies between decisions one by one. Ask about requirements, edge cases, user experience, data models, and failure modes. Do not write a plan document or code until I say we are aligned.

title: Lock Down Your AI Agent in 60 Seconds

what:
OpenClaw (the open-source AI agent) had no security guardrails by default — exposed keys, unrestricted file access, uncontrolled network activity. NVIDIA's NemoClaw wraps it in a sandboxed runtime called OpenShell that enforces network, filesystem, and privacy policies.

Two commands to go from unsandboxed to locked down.
why: The sandbox enforces three boundaries that OpenClaw lacked by default: network access (only approved endpoints), filesystem access (only specified directories), and privacy policies (what data can leave the machine). You get agent capability without the risk surface.
how:
1. Open terminal (ask Claude what a terminal is if needed)
2. Paste the curl command above — it handles full setup
3. Run the two connect commands to start your sandboxed agent
4. No-terminal option: Deploy NemoClaw on Brev — NVIDIA hosts it for $0.13/hour, one click, no setup

# Install NemoClaw
curl -fsSL https://nvidia.com/nemoclaw.sh | bash

# Connect to your sandboxed agent
nemoclaw my-assistant connect
openclaw tui

title: Make a Permission Map Before Connecting AI to Anything Sensitive
summary: Before you connect an AI tool to sensitive data — email, bank accounts, files, CRM, calendar — make it write you a permission receipt. A permission map is a checklist of what the AI can see, what it can change, what it stores, and how yo…
what:
Before you connect an AI tool to sensitive data — email, bank accounts, files, CRM, calendar — make it write you a permission receipt. A permission map is a checklist of what the AI can see, what it can change, what it stores, and how you disconnect it.

The key distinction: reading your bank transactions is one risk. Moving money, changing subscriptions, or storing private details in chat history is another.
why: Most people don't know what they've consented to when they click "Allow" on an OAuth screen. This forces the AI to enumerate the actual permissions before you grant them — turning implicit risk into explicit, reviewable choices.
how:
1. Run this before connecting email, files, calendars, CRMs, bank accounts, or workplace tools
2. The output is your permission receipt — save it somewhere
3. Check step 6 (disconnect instructions) before you need it, not after
4. Save as a Claude Skill so you can trigger it with `/permission-check` before any new connection
prompt:
You are my AI permissions auditor. Before I connect this tool or account, create a permission map.

Tool/account I'm considering connecting: [paste tool]
Data involved: [banking / email / calendar / files / CRM / other]

Give me:
1. What the AI can read.
2. What the AI can change or trigger.
3. What data may be stored in chat history, logs, or third-party systems.
4. What could go wrong in a normal workday.
5. The safest way to use it.
6. The exact steps I should take to disconnect or delete access later.

Write this for a non-technical person. Be specific and practical.

title: Make AI Show Its Work Receipt
summary: After any important AI task, ask the model to produce a "work receipt" — a structured accounting of what was actually completed, how long it would have taken manually, what you still had to review, and whether it was worth using AI at all.
what: After any important AI task, ask the model to produce a "work receipt" — a structured accounting of what was actually completed, how long it would have taken manually, what you still had to review, and whether it was worth using AI at all.
why: It's easy to confuse AI activity with AI value. A long output doesn't mean a useful output. The work receipt forces the model to be conservative about what counts as "done" (no drafts, unused ideas, or partial work), making it easier to measure whether AI is actually saving time or just generating plausible-looking output. Cognition built a similar accountability system into its Devin guarantee.
how: Run this prompt at the end of any substantial AI work session. Compare the AI's claims against what you actually shipped.
prompt:
Review the work we just completed and create an AI work receipt.

Include:
1. Finished output: What was actually completed?
2. Human baseline: How long would this likely take me manually?
3. AI-assisted time: How long did this take with you?
4. Review required: What did I still need to check, rewrite, or fix?
5. Risk: What could be wrong, incomplete, or misleading?
6. Final value estimate: Was this a small assist, a major time saver, or not worth using AI for?

Be conservative. Do not count drafts, ideas, or unused output as completed work.

title: Make ChatGPT Judge Its Own Work Before You See It
summary: Use an LLM-as-a-judge loop with Codex's /goal command. Instead of giving Codex a task and hoping for the best, you first ask the model to define what "good enough" looks like — then it works in a loop checking its own output against thos…
what: Use an LLM-as-a-judge loop with Codex's /goal command. Instead of giving Codex a task and hoping for the best, you first ask the model to define what "good enough" looks like — then it works in a loop checking its own output against those criteria before reporting back to you.
why: Most AI failures happen because there's no clear success criteria. When you ask Codex to define and then enforce its own acceptance criteria, it self-corrects rather than stopping at "looks done." The loop continues until the work measurably meets the goal — not just when the model runs out of ideas.
how: Use in Codex or Claude Code with any task that has a measurable outcome: dashboard performance, document cleanup, bug fixes, code optimization. Works especially well with /goal prefix.
prompt:
/goal

Work toward this outcome:
[describe the task]

Before starting, define what "good enough" means for this task.

Create 3-5 success criteria Codex can keep checking while it works. Include any hard requirements, tests, files, constraints, style rules, performance targets, or user-facing behavior that must be preserved.

Then work in a loop:
1. Make the next useful improvement.
2. Judge the result against the success criteria.
3. Identify the biggest remaining gap.
4. Continue until the work meets the goal.

Return:
- The final result
- The success criteria you used
- The biggest change you made

title: Make Claude Prove the Work Before You Trust the Run
summary: Boris Cherny's framework for running Claude autonomously for hours or days: use auto mode, /goal or /loop to keep the agent moving, and require end-to-end self-verification before reporting "done." The agent doesn't just claim it finishe…
what: Boris Cherny's framework for running Claude autonomously for hours or days: use auto mode, /goal or /loop to keep the agent moving, and require end-to-end self-verification before reporting "done." The agent doesn't just claim it finished — it tests the running result.
why: Agents fail in two ways: they stop too early, or they report "done" when they haven't actually verified the output works. Requiring behavioral verification (run the web app in a browser, test in a simulator, start the full service) before reporting back catches both failure modes. "Done" means the user-facing behavior works, not that the code compiled.
how: Use in Claude Code for any long-horizon task: web work, mobile features, backend changes. Start with auto mode, set a /goal, specify the verification method, then let it run.
prompt:
Run this as a long-horizon task.

Task: [describe the work]

Use auto-approved permissions only for safe project actions.
Use /goal or /loop to keep working until the outcome is complete.
If the task is too large, create a dynamic workflow and split it into sub-agents.

Do not report "done" until you self-verify end to end:
- Web: test in the browser
- Mobile: test in an iOS or Android simulator MCP
- Backend: start the full service and run the relevant checks

At the end, give me:
1. What changed
2. How you verified it
3. What risks remain

title: Make Claude Run Your Adobe Stack
