title: Make Stunning AI Videos With Google Flow (Without Burning All Your Credits)
summary: Google Flow is a powerful AI video tool, but most people open it once, feel overwhelmed, and close it. This skill gives you the intentional workflow: always start with images, edit them, then turn into video, then stitch into scenes — be…
what: Google Flow is a powerful AI video tool, but most people open it once, feel overwhelmed, and close it. This skill gives you the intentional workflow: always start with images, edit them, then turn into video, then stitch into scenes — because image generation is cheaper on credits.
why: Starting with images lets you nail the look and character consistency before spending video credits. The reference image system maintains visual continuity across shots. The timeline editor lets you trim and assemble without re-shooting. Working image → video → scene keeps costs manageable on Pro (1,000 credits/month) or Ultra (10,000/month) plans.

what: Use Google Gemini Omni (built into the Gemini app) to create AI videos directly from text prompts — no separate software, no timeline editor, no technical skills required. You can also add yourself as an avatar by scanning a QR code.

title: Make Your Agent Write Its Own Goal
summary: Before asking an agent to do the work, ask it to define its own /goal — including separate goals for any sub-agents it spawns. The model drafts the target; you tighten the constraints before execution begins.
what: Before asking an agent to do the work, ask it to define its own /goal — including separate goals for any sub-agents it spawns. The model drafts the target; you tighten the constraints before execution begins.
why: Pietro Schirano's insight: agents drift into weird edge cases without human-set boundaries. If the model writes the goal itself, it surfaces assumptions you might miss. You review and approve before any work starts, turning autonomous execution into human-reviewed autonomy. Sub-agents that have their own explicit goals stay on track better than those operating under vague inherited instructions.
how: Use in Codex or Claude Code with any multi-step task. The key step is "do not start until I approve the /goal" — this keeps you in control while delegating the planning work to the model.
prompt:
Before starting, write your own /goal for this task.

Task: [describe the task]
Context: [paste files, docs, requirements, or links]
Constraints: [scope limits, style rules, deadlines, things to avoid]
Definition of done: [what success looks like]

Return:
1. Your main /goal.
2. 3-5 success criteria.
3. Boundaries you should not cross.
4. If you spawn helper agents, write a separate /goal for each one.
5. Ask me to approve or edit the goals before execution.

Do not start until I approve the /goal.

title: Make Your CLI Agent-Friendly With These 10 Design Principles
summary: If you ship any command-line tool — for a SaaS product, an internal tool, or an open-source library — there's a real chance an AI agent is going to call it before a human does. Most CLIs were designed for humans typing at a terminal: int…
what: If you ship any command-line tool — for a SaaS product, an internal tool, or an open-source library — there's a real chance an AI agent is going to call it before a human does. Most CLIs were designed for humans typing at a terminal: interactive prompts, ANSI colors, friendly error messages. All of that is friction for an agent. This skill covers 10 design principles (from Cloudflare and HeyGen's CLI design) for building CLIs that work for both humans AND agents.
why: The fastest way to find your CLI's weaknesses is to let an agent use it. Wherever the agent gets stuck or has to ask for help is a place your CLI is failing the agent test. Interactive prompts hang agents. ANSI color codes corrupt structured output. Vague error messages leave agents guessing. Each of these 10 principles directly removes one of these failure modes.
how:
**The table-stakes principles (must-haves):**
- No interactive prompts that block input by default
- A `--json` flag for structured output
- Error messages with structured "what failed, why, what to try next"

**The advanced principles (separates good from great):**
- Consistent verbs: `list`, `get`, `create`, `update`, `delete` everywhere
- `--wait` for async operations (no polling required)
- Idempotent commands (agents retry — idempotency makes that safe)
- Specific non-zero exit codes (agents branch on exit codes)

**The agent test:** Give an agent a multi-step task that uses your CLI and watch where it gets stuck. That's almost always a design flaw, not an agent flaw.
prompt:
You're auditing the [CLI name] CLI for agent-friendliness.

Walk through this 10-principle checklist for the CLI. For each principle,
check: does the CLI satisfy it? If yes, give an example command that
demonstrates it. If no, describe what would need to change.

Principles:
1. Non-interactive defaults — no prompts that block waiting for input
2. Structured JSON output — a --json flag that emits machine-parseable output
3. Actionable errors — what failed, why, and what to try next (in structured form)
4. Consistent vocabulary — list, get, create, update, delete (same verbs everywhere)
5. --wait support for async operations — block until done (with timeout)
6. Persistent profiles — auth, default region, default project set once
7. Idempotent commands — re-running shouldn't break things
8. Discoverable schemas — list all commands, flags, and output formats programmatically
9. Stable exit codes — 0 for success; specific non-zero codes for specific failures
10. Streaming-friendly — support pagination or streaming for large outputs

End with a prioritized list of the 3 highest-impact changes the CLI team
should make to improve agent compatibility.

title: Map Who Gets the Upside
summary: Before pitching any AI change at work, map who gets the upside. This stakeholder upside map is the fastest way to find the person who will quietly block your "efficiency" project — because to them, it looks like more work, more surveilla…
what: Before pitching any AI change at work, map who gets the upside. This stakeholder upside map is the fastest way to find the person who will quietly block your "efficiency" project — because to them, it looks like more work, more surveillance, or fewer jobs. It's also useful for automations, new agents, data projects, and team-wide AI rollouts.
why: Resistance to AI projects is often framed as "people hate change" — but most of the time, people hate being handed someone else's upside as their own downside. Mapping gains, losses, and proof requirements before you pitch uncovers the real blockers and gives you concrete bargaining chips. The reverse prompt (argue against it from the opponent's perspective) surfaces the strongest objections faster than any meeting.
how: Run the stakeholder map prompt before any AI change that affects more than one team. Run the opposition prompt to pressure-test your plan.
prompt:
I'm considering this AI project: [describe the project].

Create a stakeholder upside map with these columns:
1. Stakeholder group
2. What they gain if this works
3. What they might fear or lose
4. What proof would make them trust it
5. What ownership or benefit-sharing would make the change feel fair
6. What are the downsides if this goes wrong (and how to mitigate them)
7. What's the first conversation I should have with each stakeholder

Include: employees, managers, customers, legal/compliance, IT/security, and any affected community or partner group.

---

Then: Act as the person most likely to oppose this project.
Write the strongest good-faith argument against it.
Focus on risks, incentives, hidden costs, trust issues, and what (if anything) would make you support it.

title: The Microsoft Copilot Power User Guide (When Copilot Is All You Can Use)
summary: If the only AI you can use at work is Microsoft Copilot, you're not left out — Wave 3 of Microsoft 365 Copilot transformed it from a chat assistant into a full agentic platform. Copilot now supports Claude directly inside Copilot Chat, m…
what: If the only AI you can use at work is Microsoft Copilot, you're not left out — Wave 3 of Microsoft 365 Copilot transformed it from a chat assistant into a full agentic platform. Copilot now supports Claude directly inside Copilot Chat, multi-model outputs, and agentic execution across your entire Microsoft 365 account. This is the power user guide for getting the most out of it.
why: Copilot's actual superpower vs. standalone ChatGPT or Claude is Work IQ: it already knows your emails, meetings, files, and org chart via Microsoft Graph. You never have to upload context. For anything that needs your work context, Copilot wins. Users don't even need separate Anthropic accounts — everything runs within Microsoft's existing security, identity, and compliance boundaries.
how:
**What shipped in Wave 3:**
- **Copilot Cowork** — long-running, multi-step agent built with Anthropic
- **Multi-model Chat** — select Claude directly in Copilot Chat
- **Researcher with Critique and Council** — one model drafts, a second critiques, side-by-side Claude vs. GPT comparison
- **Agent Mode in Word, Excel, PowerPoint** — iteratively build complex deliverables
- **Work IQ** — reads your emails, meetings, files, and org chart (no uploading needed)

**To unlock everything:**
1. Get your tenant into the Frontier program: Admin Center → Copilot → Copilot Frontier → enable it
2. Enable Anthropic as a subprocessor: Admin Center → Copilot → AI Providers
3. Use the right surface: Chat for quick questions, Researcher for deep analysis, Cowork for multi-step execution, Agent Mode for data in Excel

**Use ChatGPT or Claude on the side for:** creative writing, one-off research outside your company's data, and skill creation.
prompt:
Prep me for my 1:1 with [manager's name] tomorrow.

Pull:
- Any decisions or action items from our last three 1:1s (check my Teams chat history and meeting notes)
- Emails between us from the past two weeks that mention open projects
- Status of the deliverables I committed to last time (check Planner/Loop/Tasks)
- One topic I should raise proactively based on changes in our team's recent work

Output a one-page briefing doc in Word with:
1. "What's outstanding from last time" (table)
2. "Updates on my commitments" (bullets with file links)
3. "Topics I should raise" (3 items with 2-sentence context each)
4. "Questions I should ask" (5 items)

Save to my "1-1 Prep" folder in OneDrive and surface a Teams reminder at 8am tomorrow.

title: Nest CLAUDE.md at Every Level of Your File Structure
summary: Claude Code automatically loads the CLAUDE.md file from every directory above whatever file you're working on. Nest those files intelligently and every new chat starts with full context already loaded.
what:
Claude Code automatically loads the `CLAUDE.md` file from every directory above whatever file you're working on. Nest those files intelligently and every new chat starts with full context already loaded.

Taylor Pearson's four-level structure:
1. **Global** (`~/CLAUDE.md`): how you like Claude to work, voice, tools
2. **Vault** (your files folder): how your files are organized
3. **Business folder**: what the business does, clients, pricing
4. **Project folder**: goals, deadlines, people involved

Open anything inside a project and Claude walks up the tree, pulling all four into context automatically.
why: Your `CLAUDE.md` files are the difference between talking to a new employee every session and talking to one who's been with you for a year. The tree-walking means context is cumulative, not repeated.
how:
1. Create a `CLAUDE.md` at each folder level with relevant context
2. At the end of every session, run the prompt above as a `/wrap` slash command
3. Next session, Claude walks up the tree and loads all four levels automatically
4. See the `claudesidian` starter repo for a ready-made structure
prompt:
Before ending this session, update the CLAUDE.md files at the relevant folder levels (global, vault, business, project) with any decisions, preferences, or context from this session that would help the next session start smarter.

Rules:
- Be surgical. Don't bloat the files.
- Only add information that is likely to still be relevant next session.
- Put each piece of context at the lowest folder level it applies to.
- For each edit, tell me which file you changed and what you added.

title: Build a Persistent AI Notebook for Any Ongoing Project
summary: If you're working on something ongoing — job search, research project, presentation, content series, side hustle — stop treating every AI chat like a one-time conversation. Give it a home.
what:
If you're working on something ongoing — job search, research project, presentation, content series, side hustle — stop treating every AI chat like a one-time conversation. Give it a home.

Google's notebooks in Gemini let you group chats, files, and custom instructions into one place. Drop in your docs, PDFs, notes, and prior chats, then keep building from there. The notebooks sync with NotebookLM, so you can use features from both apps on the same material.
why: Context is the most expensive thing to rebuild in an AI conversation. Notebooks eliminate the re-explanation tax — every session starts where the last one left off.
how:
1. Create a new notebook in Gemini for any project lasting more than a day
2. Add your files and the context prompt above
3. Every chat in that notebook inherits the full context
4. Use NotebookLM for Video Overviews, quizzes, and podcasts from the same material
prompt:
I'm starting a notebook for [ongoing project]. Here's everything you need to know to be useful:

1. What this project is: [2-3 sentences]
2. My goal: [what success looks like]
3. What I've done so far: [key decisions, progress, blockers]
4. How I want you to communicate: [tone, format preferences]

I'll be adding documents to this notebook over time. Always read what's in the notebook before responding.

title: Plan Before You Code With Claude's Ultraplan Mode
summary: Ultraplan decouples thinking from doing: instead of one local agent reasoning sequentially, it spins up three exploration agents and one critique agent, points them at your synced GitHub repo, and returns a structured blueprint before a…
what:
Ultraplan decouples thinking from doing: instead of one local agent reasoning sequentially, it spins up three exploration agents and one critique agent, points them at your synced GitHub repo, and returns a structured blueprint before a single line gets written.

Your job becomes art director: review the plan, push back on what looks wrong, approve, then let execution run.

As the Stay Sassy crew put it: "The best engineers don't write the most code. They delete the most code." Ultraplan is the planning version of that — you're catching bad code before it exists.
why: Separating planning from execution means the mistakes happen on paper, not in code. Three exploration agents surface architectural options a single sequential agent would miss. The critique agent filters the noise.
how:
1. Activate Ultraplan mode in Claude Code
2. Paste the prompt with your feature and subsystem filled in
3. Review the architectural options and flag any concerns
4. Approve the plan before any code is generated
prompt:
Use Ultraplan mode to design [feature]. Before writing any code:
1. Map every file in this repo that touches [relevant subsystem]
2. List the 2-3 plausible architectures, with tradeoffs
3. Flag anything in the existing code that will fight your plan
4. Wait for my approval before generating any patches

title: PR Descriptions Are AI Training Data (If You Write Them That Way)
summary: Every AI coding session you close loses its reasoning forever. Two weeks later, nobody knows which prompt fixed the bug, what failed first, or why you chose that approach.
what:
Every AI coding session you close loses its reasoning forever. Two weeks later, nobody knows which prompt fixed the bug, what failed first, or why you chose that approach. This skill adds an "AI Context" block to every non-trivial PR description — turning pull requests into a training ground for your whole team.

The key insight: if you had to edit the AI's output, that's a signal worth capturing. It's feedback your team can turn into a permanent rule (e.g., added to `CLAUDE.md`).
why: Think of a PR description as a commit message for your AI reasoning. A commit records *what* changed. An AI-trace PR records *how* and *why* the AI got there. Teams that do this get smarter over time — every merged PR makes the next session start with more context instead of from zero. The "Manual corrections" field is especially powerful: it documents the gap between what the AI generated and what actually shipped.
how:
1. Add the template above to `.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE.md` in your repo
2. For every non-trivial AI-assisted PR, fill in all five fields before merging
3. When you find a "Reusable rule for next time," immediately add it to `CLAUDE.md`
4. Review the AI Context blocks in your team's quarterly retros — they're a window into what the model keeps getting wrong
prompt:
## AI Context

**Tool / model:** [Claude Code / Cursor / Codex / etc.]

**Key prompt that unlocked it:**
> [paste the prompt]

**What the AI tried first that didn't work:**
- [list failed approaches and why]

**Manual corrections:**
- [edits you made to the AI's output, with brief reason]

**Reusable rule for next time:**
- [one sentence your team should add to your CLAUDE.md or AGENTS.md]

title: Record a Task Once, Have Codex Solve It
summary: OpenAI's Record & Replay for Codex lets you demo a recurring workflow once and save it as a reusable skill — basically a named set of instructions Codex can run again whenever you need it. Think filing an expense report, submitting PTO,…
what: OpenAI's Record & Replay for Codex lets you demo a recurring workflow once and save it as a reusable skill — basically a named set of instructions Codex can run again whenever you need it. Think filing an expense report, submitting PTO, creating a correctly configured issue, or downloading the same report every Monday.
why: Instead of re-explaining the same task from scratch every time, you capture the steps once with full context. The recorded skill is inspectable and editable, so you get a reusable workflow rather than a mystery macro. Codex handles inputs that vary each time (dates, filenames, names) without you having to babysit the run.
how:
Requires macOS with Computer Use enabled in the Codex app.

1. Open Plugins in the Codex app
2. Hit the + menu and select "Record a skill"
3. Tell Codex your goal and what inputs may change later
4. Approve recording, perform the workflow, then stop when done
5. Ask Codex to refine the skill with your naming rules, defaults, and preferences
6. The final skill is inspectable and editable before you reuse it
prompt:
I'm about to record a reusable Codex skill.

Goal: [describe the recurring task]
Use this skill when: [when Codex should run it]
Inputs that may change each time: [dates, files, names, links, report ranges, etc.]
Success criteria: [how Codex should know the workflow is complete]
Hidden preferences to preserve: [naming rules, default fields, formatting choices, decision points]

Do not record or reuse: [passwords, secrets, private data, unrelated cleanup steps]
After the recording, draft the skill and ask me what needs to be refined before I reuse it.

title: Replace Your n8n / Zapier Workflow With a Claude Routine in 5 Minutes
summary: Claude Code Routines replace n8n, Make.com, and Zapier for most workflows. The old way: drag-and-drop nodes, credentials, variable mapping, hours of debugging.
what: Claude Code Routines replace n8n, Make.com, and Zapier for most workflows. The old way: drag-and-drop nodes, credentials, variable mapping, hours of debugging. The new way: describe what you want in plain English, hit save.
why: Routines have native connectors, scheduled triggers, and plain-English instructions — eliminating the node-mapping overhead that makes n8n intimidating. You describe the SOP; Claude figures out the implementation.
how:
1. Go to `claude.ai/code/routines` and click "New routine"
2. Name it (e.g., "Morning email triage")
3. Write the prompt like an SOP — be more precise than a normal chat since routines run hands-off
4. Pick a trigger: schedule (daily at 5 AM), webhook, or API call
5. Add connectors under Settings → Connectors (Gmail, Slack, Notion, etc.)
6. Hit "Run now" to test — if it works, walk away

**Migration tip:** Already have an n8n or Make workflow? Copy the JSON from your nodes (shift-select then Cmd+C), paste into Claude Code, and ask it to "turn this into a Claude routine." It rebuilds the whole thing in 30 seconds.
prompt: Pull my Gmail unreads. For each one, check for prior conversations with that contact. Draft a reply. Send me the drafts in Slack.

title: Review AI Code by Risk, Not Size
summary: A framework from Rahul Sengottuvelu (head of applied AI at Ramp) for reviewing AI-generated code changes. Stop treating every change the same way: a 12-line change to the login system can be more dangerous than a 1,200-line change to a s…
what: A framework from Rahul Sengottuvelu (head of applied AI at Ramp) for reviewing AI-generated code changes. Stop treating every change the same way: a 12-line change to the login system can be more dangerous than a 1,200-line change to a settings page. Classify by risk level, then apply the right review method.
why: Frontier AI models can now generate large amounts of correct code reliably. The bottleneck is safe deployment, not generation. Risk-based review directs human attention where it matters most (auth, payments, PII) while allowing empirical verification (tests, feature flags, shadow mode) for lower-risk changes — making review faster without becoming less safe.
how: Paste your AI-generated diff or describe the change. The model classifies it by risk tier and tells you exactly what to read line-by-line vs. what you can verify with tests.
prompt:
Review this AI-generated code by risk, not by size.

First, classify the change:
1. High risk: auth, identity, payments, data access, network access, PII, security, or production database writes.
2. Medium risk: business logic, user-facing behavior, integrations, or performance.
3. Low risk: UI, formatting, internal tooling, backend plumbing, or code that can be tested safely.

Then tell me:
- What could go wrong?
- What needs line-by-line review?
- What can be verified empirically with tests?
- Should this run behind a feature flag, in a sandbox, or in shadow mode before release?
- What guardrails would make this safe to merge faster?

[paste code or describe the change]

title: Run a Daily AI Smoke Test on Your Own Product
summary: Edge-case bugs in your product's onboarding flow are expensive precisely because they're hard to spot — the happy path works, the obvious failure modes are caught, and what gets through is the weird stuff that only triggers under specifi…
what:
Edge-case bugs in your product's onboarding flow are expensive precisely because they're hard to spot — the happy path works, the obvious failure modes are caught, and what gets through is the weird stuff that only triggers under specific account states or timing. This skill uses a daily automated smoke test loop that catches edge-case bugs weekly.

Inspired by Ryan Carson running daily automated smoke tests of a 28-step / 99-minute customer onboarding flow using DevinAI for ~$33/day. The math: $33/day × 30 days = $990/month — cheaper than one engineer-hour of manual QA, and you get *continuous* coverage.
why: Most teams smoke-test before a release. This loop catches the bugs introduced by third-party API changes, auth provider drift, and timing weirdness — the stuff that breaks *between* releases. Fresh accounts every time means you test the actual signup experience, not a cached logged-in state. The "don't abort on failure" rule means you see all failures in one report, not just the first one.
how:
1. Pick your most important user flow (onboarding, checkout, signup-to-first-value)
2. Write the steps as instructions for an agent (literally: "open a browser, sign up with a fresh email, click X, wait for Y, verify Z")
3. Configure DevinAI (or any agentic browser tool) to run that script daily on a schedule
4. Have the agent capture screenshots/video at each step and send a PASS/FAIL summary
5. Add new steps to the script every time you ship a feature that touches the flow
prompt:
You're running a daily smoke test of [product name]'s [flow name].

Setup:
- Use a fresh email address (format: smoke-test-YYYYMMDD@[your-domain]).
- Sign up via [signup URL].
- Walk through every step of [flow], including [list of integrations to exercise].

Verification at each step:
- Take a screenshot.
- Confirm [expected behavior].
- If anything fails, capture the error state and continue (don't abort).

Output:
- A PASS/FAIL summary email with screenshots inline.
- A short note for any step that PASSED but felt slow (>5s loading time, etc.).

title: Run a Platform Risk Audit
summary: Use this whenever your product, workflow, business, or audience depends on another company's platform — Apple, Google, Amazon, Meta, TikTok, Slack, Salesforce, or even ChatGPT itself. The goal: find where you're exposed before the platfo…
what:
Use this whenever your product, workflow, business, or audience depends on another company's platform — Apple, Google, Amazon, Meta, TikTok, Slack, Salesforce, or even ChatGPT itself.

The goal: find where you're exposed before the platform changes the rules, buries your feature, launches a competitor, or signs the same deal with someone else.

*"Partnerships feel safest right before the platform reminds you who owns the door."*
why: Platform risk is invisible until it isn't. Auditing proactively surfaces the "if X happens" scenarios while you have time to build alternatives — not after the platform pulls the rug.
how:
1. Describe your dependency on the platform in specific terms ("we distribute through the App Store" or "our CRM data lives in Salesforce")
2. Run the audit — focus on the "one dependency I should reduce first" output
3. Useful triggers: new platform partnership, platform price change, competitor getting a deal, new regulations
prompt:
Act as a platform strategy advisor.

Audit this dependency:
[DESCRIBE PRODUCT, WORKFLOW, PARTNERSHIP, OR DISTRIBUTION CHANNEL]

Find my biggest platform risks across:
1. Access: What could the platform block, limit, or delay?
2. Visibility: Could the platform bury us in the interface?
3. Economics: Could pricing, fees, or revenue share change?
4. Competition: Could the platform launch or favor a rival?
5. Data: What data do we need that the platform controls?
6. Switching costs: How hard would it be to move elsewhere?
7. Leverage: What do we control that the platform still needs?

Return:
- Top 5 risks
- Early warning signs
- A practical mitigation plan
- The one dependency I should reduce first

title: Run a Powerful AI Model on Your Laptop for Free
summary: You can run Google's Gemma 4 entirely on your MacBook — no internet, no subscription, no data leaving your machine — using LM Studio. Gemma 4 is technically a 26-billion-parameter model but only activates 4 billion at a time, so it runs…
what:
You can run Google's Gemma 4 entirely on your MacBook — no internet, no subscription, no data leaving your machine — using LM Studio.

Gemma 4 is technically a 26-billion-parameter model but only activates 4 billion at a time, so it runs at a surprisingly snappy 51 words per second on a standard MacBook Pro. Big-model quality, small-model speed, completely free.
why: Gemma 4 uses a mixture-of-experts architecture: 26B total parameters but only 4B active at once. This gives you frontier-quality reasoning at a fraction of the compute cost — which is why it runs fast on consumer hardware.
how:
1. Install LM Studio (lmstudio.ai) — free download
2. Search for `gemma-4` inside LM Studio and download (~18 GB)
3. Start the local server — it runs on your machine and responds like any AI API
4. Test with a question in the terminal or connect any AI tool to `localhost:1234`
5. Test vision by dragging in a screenshot — it describes every element on screen

**Why local AI?** No monthly bill, no data sent to any cloud, works offline, and you can run it all day without usage limits.
prompt: Describe what you see in this screenshot. Identify every element: title, layout, icons, data.

title: Run a "Sameness Detector" Pass
summary: Most AI drafts are fine. Fine is the danger.
what:
Most AI drafts are fine. Fine is the danger.

As Dan Shipper argues, AI makes yesterday's competence cheap — everyone can produce decent work. The problem is that decent work starts to repeat itself: same ideas, same examples, same section flow, same rhythm, same "smart" conclusion.

This skill runs a Sameness Detector pass before you publish. You're asking AI to find low-perplexity work — the parts that feel too predictable. This includes repeated ideas, duplicate sections, overused phrasing, flat transitions, obvious examples, and "polished" paragraphs that add no new information.

The goal is not to make the draft weird. It's to make it specific.
why: AI drafts default to the average of everything it has seen — predictable structure, generic examples, flat rhythm. This prompt audits across 8 distinct dimensions of sameness, then produces a concrete rewrite plan. The result is content that feels specific and alive rather than competently generic.
how:
1. Copy the prompt template above
2. Fill in your audience, goal, and paste your draft
3. Paste into Claude, ChatGPT, or Gemini
4. Review the diversification plan, then accept or refine the rewrite
prompt:
Run a Sameness Detector pass on this draft.

Your job is to find every place where the work feels predictable, repetitive, generic, or too similar to common AI output.

Audit for sameness across:
1. Ideas: repeated points, obvious claims, or concepts that do the same job
2. Structure: sections that follow the same setup, order, or argument shape
3. Phrasing: repeated words, sentence patterns, transitions, and summary lines
4. Examples: generic examples that could apply to anyone
5. Evidence: unsupported claims or proof points that feel interchangeable
6. Rhythm: paragraphs or bullets that sound too similar in length or cadence
7. Emotional beats: places where the tone stays flat, over-polished, or predictable
8. Usefulness: sentences that sound smart but do not help the reader decide, act, or understand

Then create a diversification plan:
- What should be cut?
- What should be combined?
- What needs a sharper example?
- What needs a more surprising angle?
- What needs more specificity from the audience, company, moment, or situation?
- Where should the structure change so the draft has more "perplexity," meaning useful surprise and variation?

Finally, rewrite the draft so it feels more specific, varied, and alive without making it longer by default.

Audience:
[paste audience]

Goal:
[paste goal]

Draft:
[paste draft]

title: Run an AI Security Gut Check
summary: You don't need to be a security engineer to ask better security questions. Use this to run a defensive "gut check" on any workflow where software, customer data, accounts, or automation are involved.
what:
You don't need to be a security engineer to ask better security questions. Use this to run a defensive "gut check" on any workflow where software, customer data, accounts, or automation are involved.

The goal is finding places where trust is being assumed — the common thread in most real-world breaches. A package install assumed the code was safe. A login system assumed partial access was safe. A security team assumed old scanning methods could catch new attack patterns.
why: Most security failures aren't sophisticated attacks — they're trust assumptions that nobody checked. This prompt systematically surfaces those assumptions across the seven most common categories, without requiring security expertise to run.
how:
1. Describe your workflow, system, or automation in plain English
2. Run the audit — focus on the "why a normal check might miss it" column
3. Useful triggers: before deploying new automation, after adding a new integration, after a security incident anywhere in your industry
prompt:
Act as a defensive security reviewer.

Review this workflow, system, or automation:
[PASTE DESCRIPTION]

Look for places where the system assumes trust too early.
Focus on:
1. User accounts and permissions
2. Third-party packages or integrations
3. API keys, tokens, and credentials
4. Automated actions that could cause damage
5. Data that should stay private
6. Approval steps before anything public or irreversible
7. Monitoring logs I should check regularly

For each risk, explain:
- What could go wrong
- Why a normal check might miss it
- The safest practical fix
- Whether this needs an expert review

Keep this defensive only. Do not provide exploit steps.

title: Run Gemma 4 Locally — Three Ways, From Easiest to Most Powerful
summary: Google's Gemma 4 is one of the most capable AI models you can run for free. Three ways to try it: On Android (easiest): Install Google AI Edge Gallery from the Play Store.
what:
Google's Gemma 4 is one of the most capable AI models you can run for free. Three ways to try it:

**On Android (easiest):** Install Google AI Edge Gallery from the Play Store. Download the Gemma 4 E2B model. You now have an offline AI that processes images, understands voice, and speaks 140+ languages — running entirely on your phone.

**On desktop via LM Studio:** Download LM Studio → search Gemma 4 → download → run. One-command local server.

**Via Ollama (one command):**
```
ollama run gemma4
```
why: Gemma 4 uses mixture-of-experts: massive parameter count, tiny active footprint. Frontier-quality output on consumer hardware, completely offline, no API costs.
how:
1. **Android**: Play Store → Google AI Edge Gallery → download Gemma 4 E2B
2. **Desktop**: Install Ollama (ollama.com) → run `ollama run gemma4`
3. **Power users**: LM Studio for a full GUI with model management
prompt: ollama run gemma4

title: Run the Same Task Through Two Models and Let Them Grade Each Other
summary: Different models make different mistakes — and you can use that to your advantage. Run any meaningful task through two different models, then have a third pass act as judge.
what:
Different models make different mistakes — and you can use that to your advantage. Run any meaningful task through two different models, then have a third pass act as judge. Models are bad at catching their own mistakes but surprisingly good at catching each other's.

Real example: DeepSeek V4 found and fixed 8 memory leaks in code Claude Opus 4.7 had written — in minutes, for about three cents. Same code, different blind spots.
why: Models are trained on their own outputs and can't see their own blind spots. A cross-model review forces each model's weaknesses into view from a different angle — the same principle behind peer review and red teams.
how:
1. Run the same task in ChatGPT and Claude
2. Paste both outputs into the template above
3. Run the judge prompt in whichever model you trust more for that task type
4. The whole loop takes about 90 seconds
prompt:
Below are two responses to the same task: [paste task].

Response A (from [Model 1]): [paste]

Response B (from [Model 2]): [paste]

Act as a strict reviewer. Identify:
1. Factual errors or hallucinations in either response.
2. Reasoning gaps or unsupported claims.
3. What each response gets right that the other misses.
4. The single strongest version, with specific edits.

Be direct. Don't hedge.

title: Run Your Whole Linear Board on Codex Autopilot With OpenAI Symphony
summary: OpenAI's open-source Symphony orchestration framework turns your Linear board into an autopilot for Codex coding agents. Every open Linear issue maps to a dedicated agent workspace.
what:
OpenAI's open-source [Symphony](https://github.com/openai/symphony) orchestration framework turns your Linear board into an autopilot for Codex coding agents. Every open Linear issue maps to a dedicated agent workspace. Symphony polls the board, spawns a Codex agent for each new ticket in an isolated git workspace, and runs the agent in non-interactive mode until it produces a PR. Each agent comments back on its ticket with CI status, complexity analysis, and a walkthrough video.

Some OpenAI teams saw a 500% increase in landed pull requests in the first three weeks of using it.
why: The bottleneck in software development used to be writing code — Codex eliminated that. The next bottleneck became context-switching between supervised agent sessions. Symphony eliminates that too. Each issue gets its own isolated filesystem. The orchestrator always refetches Linear state, so a PM moving an issue to "Cancelled" is respected immediately. You stop managing agent tabs and start managing your sprint board like a project manager with infinite headcount.
how:
1. Clone Symphony: `git clone https://github.com/openai/symphony`
2. Install: `cd symphony/elixir && mise trust && mise install`
3. Build and run: `mise exec -- mix setup && mise exec -- mix build && mise exec -- ./bin/symphony ./WORKFLOW.md`
4. Create your `WORKFLOW.md` file using the YAML front matter template above — set your Linear API key, project slug, workspace root, and Codex model
5. Open the Phoenix LiveView dashboard for real-time agent visibility
6. Manage your sprint board as you normally would; Symphony handles the rest

For a portable bootstrap (no absolute paths), use [codex-symphony](https://github.com/Citedy/codex-symphony) instead.
prompt:
---
tracker:
  kind: linear
  api_key: $LINEAR_API_KEY
  project_slug: "your-project-slug"
workspace:
  root: ~/code/workspaces
  hooks:
    after_create: |
      git clone git@github.com:your-org/your-repo.git .
agent:
  max_concurrent_agents: 10
  max_turns: 20
codex:
  command: codex app-server --model gpt-5.3-codex
---
You are working on Linear issue {{ issue.identifier }}.
Title: {{ issue.title }}
Body: {{ issue.description }}

Implement the change in this workspace, run tests, and open a PR.

title: Set Up a Fully Local Browser AI in 5 Minutes (No Cloud, No API Key)
summary: You can run a fully private, on-device AI agent inside Chrome — no API keys, no cloud, no data leaving your laptop. Nico Martin's Chrome extension loads a 2B-parameter Gemma 4 model (~500MB) into your browser via WebGPU.
what:
You can run a fully private, on-device AI agent inside Chrome — no API keys, no cloud, no data leaving your laptop. Nico Martin's [Chrome extension](https://chromewebstore.google.com/detail/transformersjs-gemma-4-br/dhaknnnkcdkjhcclchmnfdhddoehoool) loads a 2B-parameter Gemma 4 model (~500MB) into your browser via WebGPU. It can read open tabs, search your browsing history semantically, summarize the current page, and highlight DOM elements — all locally.

This is the Transformers.js + Gemma 4 + WebGPU stack: a real production-ready architecture for shipping privacy-respecting AI features inside any browser extension.
why: The extension uses semantic embeddings on your pages — it generates embeddings of headings, paragraphs, and lists, then returns only the most relevant chunks for your question. The model never sees the full DOM. Speed is ~5-10 tokens/sec on integrated GPU (faster on dedicated cards). Background service worker loads the model once and persists it across tabs, so you pay the loading cost only once per session.
how:
1. Install the [extension from the Chrome Web Store](https://chromewebstore.google.com/detail/transformersjs-gemma-4-br/dhaknnnkcdkjhcclchmnfdhddoehoool) — one click
2. First load triggers a one-time download of Gemma 4 E2B ONNX model and `all-MiniLM-L6-v2` embedding model (~500MB total, cached after that)
3. Open the side panel and chat

For more agent-like control (click/type/scroll DOM, screenshot capture), use [Gemma Gem](https://github.com/kessler/gemma-gem) instead — it adds a `run_javascript` tool and lets you choose between Gemma 4 E2B (~500MB) or E4B (~1.5GB).

**Tools available on-device:** `get_open_tabs`, `ask_website` (semantic search on current page), `find_history` (semantic search across browsing history), `summarize_tab`, `highlight_element`
prompt:
You have access to my open tabs and the current page.

Find the tab where I was reading about [topic], summarize what it says about [specific question], and tell me whether the article disagrees with what I'm currently reading.

title: Set Up OpenClaw From Scratch: Three Levels
summary: OpenClaw is an open-source AI agent (100K+ GitHub stars in a week) that lives in WhatsApp or Telegram, runs 24/7, and can change itself by writing code when it needs new abilities. Unlike ChatGPT or Claude, you own the agent and it opera…
what:
OpenClaw is an open-source AI agent (100K+ GitHub stars in a week) that lives in WhatsApp or Telegram, runs 24/7, and can change itself by writing code when it needs new abilities. Unlike ChatGPT or Claude, you own the agent and it operates continuously — not just when you open a chat.

Three levels:
- **Beginner**: Set up a to-do list and daily check-ins
- **Intermediate**: Connect email and calendar, get one morning briefing
- **Advanced**: Give it ongoing projects, let it make phone calls, build compound workflows
why: OpenClaw runs continuously and can modify itself. The morning briefing alone — checking email, calendar, and weather before you open your laptop — replaces 15 minutes of manual triage every day.
how:
1. **Laptop install**: Run the curl command above — it walks you through connecting to your messaging app (~10 minutes)
2. **24/7 server**: Deploy on Fly.io, Hetzner, or Google Cloud so it runs when your laptop is closed
3. **Hosted**: Every is building a one-click hosted version (request early access)
4. Start with the morning briefing — it's the highest-ROI first use case
5. Go proactive: "When I get a meeting invite, check for conflicts and tell me"
prompt:
# Install OpenClaw
curl -fsSL https://docs.openclaw.ai/install.sh | bash

# First tasks to text your Claw after setup:
# Morning briefing: "Every day at 8am, check my email, calendar, and weather, then send me one message with everything I need."
# To-do list: "Manage my to-do list. Every morning, send me my to-dos for the day."
# Email: "I want you to read my email. What do I need to do?"
# Proactive: "When I get a meeting invite, check for conflicts and tell me."

title: Set Up Your AI Platform in 15 Minutes
summary: The biggest unlock in AI right now has nothing to do with which model you use. It's whether you've spent 15 minutes configuring your setup.
what:
The biggest unlock in AI right now has nothing to do with which model you use. It's whether you've spent 15 minutes configuring your setup.

Both Claude and ChatGPT now have full customization stacks (skills, connectors, plugins) that turn generic chatbots into personalized workspaces. The people getting dramatically more value configured one thing: a project file that teaches the AI how their specific work gets done.

Phil Schmid (ex-Hugging Face) calls it "the most important concept in 2026" — the model is the engine, but the project file is the steering wheel.
why: Without a project file, every conversation starts from zero. With one, the AI already knows your role, your format preferences, and your recurring workflows before you type a single word.
how:
1. Open your AI tool's settings menu
2. Connect ONE tool you use daily (Google Calendar, Slack, or your CRM)
3. Install ONE plugin that matches your job function
4. Run the prompt above to create your first `CLAUDE.md` or `AGENTS.md` file
5. Add it to your main working folder — the AI loads it automatically
prompt:
Create my CLAUDE.md project file. Here's what you need to capture:

My role: [your job title and what you actually do]
My preferred output format: [how you want responses — bullets, prose, tables, etc.]
One recurring weekly process: [something you do every week where AI could help]

Based on this, write a CLAUDE.md file I can add to my main working folder. Keep it under 20 lines. Be specific, not generic.

title: Set Up Your Claude Code Like Creator Boris Cherny
summary: Boris Cherny (creator of Claude Code) and Cat Wu's workflow for using Claude Code as a tiny team rather than a fancy autocomplete box. Key principles: worktrees for parallel work, auto mode for less interruption, agent view for monitorin…
what: Boris Cherny (creator of Claude Code) and Cat Wu's workflow for using Claude Code as a tiny team rather than a fancy autocomplete box. Key principles: worktrees for parallel work, auto mode for less interruption, agent view for monitoring, and behavioral verification over test-driven development.
why: Treating each agent as a scoped worker with its own session prevents context bleed and makes parallel work safe. Auto mode reduces approval fatigue on routine steps. Turning repeated mistakes into CLAUDE.md memory means the system gets smarter over time. Behavioral verification (does it actually work for the user?) catches failures that passing tests can mask.
how:
1. Use Claude Code desktop app — it handles worktrees automatically
2. Open agent view in terminal for background agents
3. Launch one scoped task per agent with a specific job
4. Use auto mode; let agents run and switch to the next task
5. When Claude makes the same mistake twice, tell it to update CLAUDE.md
6. Make Claude verify by running the actual feature, not just tests
7. Move recurring work into /loop or /goal commands
8. Use /remote-control to check sessions from your phone
9. Keep context minimal: goal, constraints, and how to find more context
prompt:
I want to set up Claude Code using Boris Cherny's workflow for [my project].

Project: [describe what you're building]
Stack: [languages, frameworks, key files]
Recurring tasks I want to automate: [list 3-5 tasks like PR review, CI fixes, docs cleanup]

Please:
1. Suggest how to scope the first 2-3 parallel agent tasks
2. Draft a CLAUDE.md entry with my key constraints and preferences
3. Suggest /goal or /loop commands for my most repeated work
4. Tell me what "behavioral verification" should look like for this project

Start by asking me any clarifying questions about the project structure.

title: Skip the Default Claude Aesthetic in Claude Design
summary: The biggest complaint about Claude Design: every output looks the same — teal gradients, serif font, blinking status dot, container-on-container layouts. The reason is Claude Design falls back to a small set of default presets when you g…
what:
The biggest complaint about Claude Design: every output looks the same — teal gradients, serif font, blinking status dot, container-on-container layouts. The reason is Claude Design falls back to a small set of default presets when you give it a loose prompt.

The fix: give it a design system to mimic *before* asking for any screens. Upload reference screenshots, name specific design tokens, and make Claude build the design system first — then generate screens against that system.
why: "System first, screens second" is the same pattern professional designers use. Establishing tokens and primitives before generating UI means every screen inherits consistent rules — instead of Claude making fresh aesthetic decisions on each prompt.
how:
1. Gather 3-5 screenshots of apps whose design you want to borrow
2. Upload them with the prompt above
3. Review the design system Claude produces before generating any screens
4. Then generate screens against the system — use the Comment tool for surgical edits
5. Alternatively: install the open-source `ui-ux-pro-max` skill (55K+ GitHub stars) for 50+ styles, 161 color palettes, and 99 UX guidelines
prompt:
Before we design anything, build me a design system based on these references:
[upload 3-5 screenshots of apps you like]

Define tokens for: color palette (name each one), typography scale, spacing scale, border-radius values, shadow depths, and component primitives (buttons, inputs, cards).

When you're done, show me the system as a style guide page. Don't generate any screens yet. I'll tell you when we're ready.

title: Stop Your AI Workflows From Silently Breaking in Production
summary: You shipped an AI workflow that worked great in testing. A week later, the model provider updated weights, an upstream API started returning new fields, or your prompts started hitting an edge case nobody anticipated — and now your workf…
what:
You shipped an AI workflow that worked great in testing. A week later, the model provider updated weights, an upstream API started returning new fields, or your prompts started hitting an edge case nobody anticipated — and now your workflow is producing subtly worse output. Nobody flags it because the workflow still runs. This is *silent drift*, and it's the most expensive failure mode for AI in production.

Based on n8n's Production AI Playbook by Elvis Saravia: production AI workflows need monitoring the way production code needs monitoring, but the metrics are different.
why: Structural checks are vastly underutilized. They're cheap (just check if required fields exist) but they catch 80% of regressions. Teams default to expensive LLM-as-Judge evals when the simpler check would have caught the problem first. The combination — structural checks for fast detection, LLM-as-Judge for quality assessment — gives you both speed and depth without paying for every check at the expensive rate.
how:
**The four layers of evaluation:**

1. **Built-in metrics**: Latency, cost per run, success rate, output length distribution. Track by default; alert on anomalies.

2. **LLM-as-a-Judge scoring**: Spin up a separate model whose only job is to score the output quality of your production model. Tell it the criteria; it grades each run on a scale.

3. **Structural checks**: Did the output match the expected schema? Did all required fields appear? Cheap, deterministic, catches a huge fraction of regressions.

4. **Tool-use validation**: When your agent calls tools, are the inputs sane? Are the outputs being used correctly downstream?

**How to apply this in n8n (or anywhere):**
- Sample 5-10% of production traffic into an evaluation pipeline
- Run all four layers above against the sample
- Trigger alerts when any single metric drops below threshold OR when multiple metrics drift together
- Review failing traces weekly; promote learnings into your prompts/tests
prompt:
You're a quality judge for an AI production workflow.

Workflow purpose: [describe what the production AI is supposed to do]
Input: [the input that was given]
Output: [the output that was produced]
Expected schema/format: [if applicable]

Score 1-10 on:
- Accuracy (does the output match what's actually true given the input?)
- Completeness (does the output cover everything the prompt asked for?)
- Format adherence (does the output match the expected schema/format?)
- Tool-use sanity (if tools were called, were the inputs reasonable?)

For any score below 8, explain what's wrong and what would need to change.

title: Stop Asking Which AI Is Best. Ask Which One Fits Your Workflow.
summary: A routing framework from Patrick Giwa for deciding which AI tool fits which task — not based on benchmarks, but based on where your work already lives. Copilot for Microsoft 365 workflows, Gemini for Google Workspace, Claude for heavy re…
what: A routing framework from Patrick Giwa for deciding which AI tool fits which task — not based on benchmarks, but based on where your work already lives. Copilot for Microsoft 365 workflows, Gemini for Google Workspace, Claude for heavy reasoning and large-document analysis.
why: The best AI isn't always the most popular one — it's the one that integrates into how your team already works. Routing the right task to the right model reduces friction, improves output quality, and stops you from fighting the tool's native environment. Many enterprise companies block ChatGPT but allow Copilot; knowing this saves real time.
how:
Three rules:
- **Copilot** if your team runs on Microsoft 365 — it's native inside Word, Excel, Outlook, Teams, and GitHub
- **Gemini** if your work lives in Google Workspace — Gmail, Docs, Sheets, Drive, Meet
- **Claude** when the task requires reasoning across large material: legal review, research synthesis, long-document analysis, complex judgment calls
prompt:
Help me route this task to the right AI tool.

Task description: [describe what you need to accomplish]
My available tools: [list which AI tools you have access to — Copilot, Gemini, Claude, ChatGPT, etc.]
Where this work happens: [Microsoft 365 / Google Workspace / standalone app / code editor]
Time sensitivity: [quick answer vs. deep analysis]
Data sensitivity: [can data leave your environment? Any compliance requirements?]
Task type: [writing / coding / research / summarizing / analyzing data / generating images]

Recommend which AI tool to use, why, and the best way to frame the task for that tool.

title: Stop Losing Context When Your AI Session Hits the Wall
summary: If you've run a long AI session, you know the moment: two hours in, the model is finally good, and then you hit the context limit. Most people copy a "where I left off" note into a fresh session manually.
what:
If you've run a long AI session, you know the moment: two hours in, the model is finally good, and then you hit the context limit. Most people copy a "where I left off" note into a fresh session manually. It works, but it's lossy and tedious.

Matt Pocock built and open-sourced a `/handoff` skill that automates this. It's a SKILL.md (a reusable instruction set you attach to a Claude project) that compacts the session into a clean handoff doc: context, goals, artifacts produced, suggested next steps.
why: Agents lose context over long tasks. A handoff document acts like a structured memory transfer — capturing not just what was done, but why decisions were made, so the next session can pick up with full context instead of starting cold.
how:
1. Grab the SKILL.md from Matt Pocock's skills repo
2. Add it to your Claude project (Settings → Skills → Add)
3. Type `/handoff` when you're about to run out of context
4. Copy the resulting Markdown into a fresh session

Works for any long task, not just coding: research, writing, strategy — anywhere value compounds across turns.
prompt:
/handoff

Compact this session into a clean handoff document. Include:
- Current goal and sub-goals
- Context that took us multiple turns to establish
- Artifacts produced so far (with links/paths)
- Decisions made and why
- What's blocking, if anything
- Suggested next steps for whoever picks this up

title: Stop Over-Explaining: Give AI Examples Instead of Instructions
summary: A lot of people write their AI a mini novel and hope it somehow nails the tone, format, and vibe. Usually, it doesn't.
what:
A lot of people write their AI a mini novel and hope it somehow nails the tone, format, and vibe. Usually, it doesn't.

If you want a summary in a certain style, a LinkedIn post that sounds like you, or notes cleaned up a specific way — showing one strong example usually works better than a long list of instructions.

AI is great at spotting patterns. A good example quietly teaches tone, structure, length, and detail all at once.
why: The best AI users often aren't giving better instructions — they're giving the model a better pattern to follow. One strong example can teach tone, structure, length, and detail that would take paragraphs to describe in instructions.
how:
1. Find one example of output you actually like (a post, summary, email, analysis)
2. Paste it after "Here's an example of what I want:"
3. Add your new content after "Now do the same thing for:"
4. Skip the instructions — let the pattern do the work

**Works especially well for:**
- LinkedIn posts that sound like you, not AI
- Summaries in a specific house style
- Emails that match your tone with a specific person
- Meeting notes cleaned up a particular way
prompt:
Here's an example of what I want: [paste example]

Now do the same thing for: [paste your new content]

title: Stop Saving Prompts in Docs — Use Claude Skills Instead
summary: If you've been copy-pasting your best prompts from a Google Doc every time you need them, Claude Skills is the upgrade. Reusable instruction sets you install once and trigger with a single slash command — in any chat.
what:
If you've been copy-pasting your best prompts from a Google Doc every time you need them, Claude Skills is the upgrade. Reusable instruction sets you install once and trigger with a single slash command — in any chat.

Alex Pereira built six of them to run his entire content workflow (hook generator, image creator, video generator, thumbnail builder, face lock, caption writer) — all fired with one word.

**Alex's rule for when to build a skill:** the task must be recurring, structured, and have a consistent output format. All three? Build it. One or two? Keep it as a saved prompt.
why: The friction of finding and copy-pasting a saved prompt is enough to make you not use it. A slash command removes that friction entirely — the skill is always one word away, in any context.
how:
1. In Claude, click **Customize** → **+** → **Create Skill**
2. Structure it in three parts: (1) description + when Claude should trigger it, (2) instructions using the template above, (3) examples of good output
3. Save it — then type `/[skill name]` in any chat to activate
4. Start with your most repeated prompt (the one you've copy-pasted more than 10 times)
prompt:
When I type /[skill name], do the following:

Instructions: [What you want Claude to do]
Format: [Output shape — e.g. "10 hooks, numbered list, under 15 words each"]
Voice rules: [Any style/tone requirements]
Examples of good output:
1. [Example 1]
2. [Example 2]

title: Teach Claude to Talk Like a Caveman (and Cut Your API Bill by 75%)
summary: For developers paying per API token only — this won't help regular Claude chat subscribers. A viral r/ClaudeAI post showed that forcing Claude into caveman-speak slashes output tokens dramatically.
what:
**For developers paying per API token only** — this won't help regular Claude chat subscribers.

A viral r/ClaudeAI post showed that forcing Claude into caveman-speak slashes output tokens dramatically. Output tokens cost 5x more than input on Claude Sonnet ($15 vs $3 per million tokens), so cutting verbose output hits where it hurts most.

The rules: 3-6 word sentences, no filler, drop articles, run tools first then stop.
why: LLMs naturally generate verbose outputs because that's what's rewarded in training. Explicit brevity constraints override the default verbosity — cutting output tokens without losing the actual information you need.
how:
1. Add these rules to your system prompt
2. Works best for coding agents and tool-calling workflows where you don't need prose
3. Run tool → get result → stop. No narration between steps.
4. Monitor your actual token usage — input context (conversation history, files) can dominate in long sessions regardless
prompt:
RESPONSE RULES:
- 3-6 word sentences max
- No preamble, no pleasantries
- Drop articles (a, an, the)
- Tool first, result, stop. No narration.
- Skip confirmations. Just do.

title: The 5-Step Framework for Building Reliable AI Agents
summary: Most people build AI agents by telling them what to do. Yuri Kramarz (Principal Engineer at Cisco Talos) says that's exactly why most agents fail.
what:
Most people build AI agents by telling them what to do. Yuri Kramarz (Principal Engineer at Cisco Talos) says that's exactly why most agents fail. His fix: give your agent an identity, define its boundaries, and force it through a structured thinking loop.

Five structural rules:
1. **Identity**: One precise sentence of purpose ("I analyze customer feedback to surface product improvement opportunities")
2. **Boundaries**: Write what it does AND what it won't do — "I summarize documents. I will not make recommendations."
3. **Observe → Reflect → Act**: Facts first, synthesis second, output last
4. **Validation checkpoint**: Before delivering output, ask: Am I sure? What would make this wrong? Is this complete?
5. **Honest limitations**: Build in explicitly — "I cannot analyze images" / "complex legal questions require review"
why: Clarity beats clever. Agents fail when their scope is fuzzy, their boundaries undefined, and their output validation missing. This framework adds all three with minimal overhead.
how:
1. Fill in the identity sentence first — if you can't write it in one sentence, the agent's scope is too broad
2. Write the "do not" list before the "do" list — it clarifies scope faster
3. The Observe/Reflect/Act loop prevents premature output
4. Ship the limitations honestly — they build trust, not doubt
prompt:
You are [one sentence identity].

You do: [specific list of what this agent handles]
You do NOT: [specific list of what this agent refuses to do]

For every task, work through:
1. OBSERVE: What are the facts in front of me?
2. REFLECT: What do they mean together? What's missing?
3. ACT: Based on that synthesis, what's the right output?

Before delivering any output, ask yourself: Am I sure? What would make this wrong? Is this complete and accurate?

Limitations to disclose when relevant: [list your agent's real limitations]

title: The Architect Approach to AI Coding
summary: Most people ask AI to generate an entire project at once. The result: a 10,000-line blob that's hard to review, test, or debug when something breaks.
what:
Most people ask AI to generate an entire project at once. The result: a 10,000-line blob that's hard to review, test, or debug when something breaks.

AI researcher Demetri Spanos's fix: think like an architect. Break the work into modules first, define the interfaces, then generate one module at a time — 200-2,000 lines each — and review before moving to the next.

A 10-20% productivity improvement in software engineering is already enormous. The goal isn't 10x speed; it's 10% better quality, consistently.
why: By constraining each generation to a single module with clear boundaries, you get code that's reviewable, testable, and maintainable. You catch errors per-module instead of debugging a 10,000-line blob — and you can actually keep track of what you've built when something inevitably breaks.
how:
1. Describe your project and run the planning prompt above first
2. Review the module breakdown — push back on anything that feels too large or poorly bounded
3. Build one module at a time, reviewing and testing each before proceeding
4. Keep modules to 200-2,000 lines; smaller is better for review
prompt:
I'm building [describe your project]. Before writing any code, help me:

1. Break this into separate modules (aim for 10-20 components)
2. Define how each module talks to the others (APIs, data flow)
3. Estimate the approximate size of each module (ballpark lines of code)
4. Identify which modules can be built independently

Then generate each module one at a time, separately. Each module should be 200-2,000 lines. Do not generate the next module until I've reviewed the current one.

title: The Fable Prompt
summary: The problem with learning hard concepts from AI: you read a definition, nod, close the tab, and by Tuesday you remember nothing. Amanda Askell (Anthropic's in-house philosopher) shared this prompt technique: instead of asking Claude to e…
what:
The problem with learning hard concepts from AI: you read a definition, nod, close the tab, and by Tuesday you remember nothing.

Amanda Askell (Anthropic's in-house philosopher) shared this prompt technique: instead of asking Claude to explain a concept, ask it to write a fable that *embodies* the concept, with the reveal saved for the end. Your brain processes story far more durably than definitions.
why: Narrative structure creates episodic memory that definitions don't. By the time the concept is named, you've already built intuition for it through the story — which is why the explanation hits harder and sticks longer.
how:
1. Pick any field: philosophy, economics, quantum mechanics, evolutionary biology, linguistics
2. Replace `[YOUR FIELD HERE]` with your chosen field
3. Let Claude pick the concept — or specify one if you prefer
4. Read the fable, then read the reveal
5. Example: try it with game theory and watch how much harder "Nash equilibrium" hits when three merchants in a village discover it the hard way
prompt: I want you to select a concept at about the graduate-student level from the field of [YOUR FIELD HERE]. Then indirectly explain this concept completely by writing a fable. Structure it so that only toward the very end do readers gradually realize what the concept actually is. After the story, add a section that clearly articulates the concept you just conveyed.

title: The Opus 4.7 + GPT-5.5 Handoff
summary: Dan Shipper found the cheat code for senior-engineer-level coding: use Opus 4.7 to write the plan, then hand that plan to GPT-5.5 to execute. On Every's internal Senior Engineer benchmark: - Human senior engineers: 80-90 - This combo: 62…
what:
Dan Shipper found the cheat code for senior-engineer-level coding: use Opus 4.7 to write the plan, then hand that plan to GPT-5.5 to execute.

On Every's internal Senior Engineer benchmark:
- Human senior engineers: 80-90
- This combo: **62.5/100**
- Opus 4.7 alone: low 30s
- GPT-5.5 alone: low-to-mid 40s

Why it works: Opus 4.7 writes tight, contract-style plans (exact file counts, line limits, first-principles design). That terseness is what gives GPT-5.5 the confidence to delete files, rewrite from scratch, and carry a multi-hour refactor through to completion.
why: Opus 4.7 is a strong planner; GPT-5.5 is a bold executor. Separating planning from execution — and matching each task to the right model — produces better results than either model doing both.
how:
1. Open Claude with Opus 4.7 selected
2. Ask it to write a rewrite plan for your target codebase
3. Paste that plan into Codex or ChatGPT with GPT-5.5 selected
4. Use the prompt above to execute
5. Tip: if you only have GPT-5.5, add explicit "here's what good looks like" targets (e.g., "this file should be ~100 lines when done") to draw out its boldness
prompt: Here is a plan written by a senior engineer for rewriting this codebase from first principles. Execute it faithfully. Do not patch around the existing code: delete what the plan says to delete, rewrite what it says to rewrite, and match its conceptual structure exactly. Carry the plan through from start to finish.

title: The "Rule of Two" for Agent Security
summary: Agents can do three things: access your files, access the internet, and execute code. NVIDIA's internal framework: only let them do two at a time.
what:
Agents can do three things: access your files, access the internet, and execute code. NVIDIA's internal framework: only let them do two at a time.

- **File access + code execution** = internet access is the vulnerability (malware from web runs against your private data)
- **File access + internet** = need to know exact scope of what it can do
- **Internet + code execution** = no access to your sensitive files
why: The three capabilities form a risk triangle. Any two are manageable; all three creates the intersection of network access, data access, and code execution that makes agent compromise catastrophic. Restricting one leg of the triangle limits blast radius to something recoverable.
how:
1. List what capabilities your agent currently has or needs
2. Run the prompt above to get a security recommendation
3. Implement the restriction before deploying
4. For extra security: run agents in an isolated environment (NVIDIA uses sandboxed VMs completely off the corporate network)
5. Build CLIs instead of raw API access — CLIs pre-define exactly what commands the agent can run
prompt:
You are a security review assistant. I'm deploying an AI agent that will have access to [describe: files/internet/code execution].

Using the "Rule of Two" framework (agents should only have 2 of 3 capabilities: file access, internet access, code execution):
1. Identify which capability I should restrict
2. Explain why that restriction reduces the most risk
3. Suggest specific sandboxing measures for my setup
4. List the minimum permissions the agent actually needs to do its job

title: The Skill That Separates AI Power Users From Everyone Else
summary: Anthropic research shows a growing skills gap: experienced AI users are pulling dramatically ahead. The biggest difference?
what:
Anthropic research shows a growing skills gap: experienced AI users are pulling dramatically ahead. The biggest difference? Power users give AI better context *before* asking it to work.

Instead of shouting tasks at a stranger, think of it like onboarding a new hire — brief them on who you are, what you're working on, what good looks like, and what to avoid.
why: AI outputs are bounded by the context they receive. Most people provide the task but not the frame. Adding role, project context, success criteria, and constraints turns a generic answer into something calibrated to your actual situation.
how:
1. Fill in all five fields before making your request
2. Save as a Project Context for recurring work in the same area
3. Or save as a reusable Skill that explains your workflow to any AI agent
4. The more specific the "what good looks like" section, the better the output
prompt:
You are helping me with [specific project/task]. Here's what you need to know:

About me: [Your role, expertise level, industry]
The project: [What you're working on and why]
What good looks like: [Examples, tone, format, length]
Constraints: [What to avoid, word limits, style rules]
Success criteria: [How I'll judge if this is useful]

With this context, please [your actual request].

title: The Spreadsheet Whisperer: Prompts That Actually Understand Your Data
summary: As of 2026 you have two genuinely powerful paths for AI spreadsheet work: Claude for Excel (better for multi-tab workbook analysis and formula reasoning) and ChatGPT for Excel (better for financial modeling and live market data). This sk…
what: As of 2026 you have two genuinely powerful paths for AI spreadsheet work: Claude for Excel (better for multi-tab workbook analysis and formula reasoning) and ChatGPT for Excel (better for financial modeling and live market data). This skill covers the 4-step analysis framework and the prompts that actually get deep results from your data.
why:
When you upload .xlsx to ChatGPT directly, it captures static values and loses all formula logic. Claude for Excel reads live formulas, provides clickable cell references, makes direct edits while preserving dependencies, and navigates across dozens of tabs. The "before/after formula" rule in the prompt means you can audit every change visually before it's applied. The "Questions I should ask" section forces the model to surface what the data *implies* but doesn't *answer*.

**Security warning:** Both add-ins warn that prompt injection attacks can hide malicious instructions inside spreadsheet cells. Only use with trusted spreadsheets — never with downloaded vendor templates from the internet.
how:
**Setup for Claude for Excel (2 minutes):**
1. Open Excel → Home → Add-ins → search "Claude by Anthropic" → install
2. Press `Ctrl+Alt+C` (Windows) or `Ctrl+Option+C` (Mac) to toggle the Claude sidebar
3. Sign in with your paid Claude account

**The 4-step analysis framework:**
1. **Explore**: "Tell me what's in this workbook. Which tabs relate to each other? Flag anything unusual."
2. **Diagnose**: "Why did [metric] change? Show me the formula chain that drives it."
3. **Predict**: "If [variable] changes by X%, show me the downstream effect across all tabs."
4. **Prescribe**: "Build a summary sheet with the three recommendations a finance director would make from this data."

**The audit trick**: If a financial assumption doesn't update the right downstream cell, highlight it and ask "Why isn't this cell linked to [x]?" The value of AI in Excel isn't in the build — it's in the audit.

**Claude vs. ChatGPT for Excel:**
- Claude: stronger at multi-tab complex workbooks, explaining formula chain behavior
- ChatGPT: stronger at live financial data from FactSet/S&P/LSEG, building models from scratch
prompt:
I have [describe your dataset: rows, columns, what each tab contains].

Step 1: Create a dashboard in a new sheet called "Executive Summary" with key trends.
- Use Python scripts for any calculation Excel formulas can't handle cleanly
- Include 3-5 KPI cards at the top (use conditional formatting for up/down arrows)
- Add 2-3 charts: one time series, one comparison, one distribution
- Add a "What changed vs. last period" callout box

Step 2: Format static input cells in BLUE and formula-linked cells in BLACK.
This lets me audit your work visually.

Step 3: Add a "Questions I should ask" section at the bottom with 3 analytical
questions this data surfaces but doesn't answer.

Before making any change to existing cells, show me the before/after formula.
Don't overwrite anything without approval.

title: This Skill Writes Prompts For You
summary: Midjourney, Claude Code, Cursor, and GPT-Image each need completely different prompt structures. The prompt-master Claude skill (free, on GitHub) auto-detects which tool you're targeting and rewrites your prompt in the right format — cat…
what: Midjourney, Claude Code, Cursor, and GPT-Image each need completely different prompt structures. The `prompt-master` Claude skill (free, on GitHub) auto-detects which tool you're targeting and rewrites your prompt in the right format — catching 35 common patterns that burn credits.
why: Each AI tool has a different "dialect" for prompts. A paragraph that works in Claude reads as noise to Midjourney. Auto-detecting the target and reformatting saves the trial-and-error loop most people go through when switching tools.
how:
1. Use this prompt directly in Claude when switching between AI tools
2. Or install the `prompt-master` skill: download SKILL.md from GitHub, add to your Claude project under Settings → Skills
3. The skill activates automatically and rewrites your prompts for the detected tool

**Covers:** Midjourney (keywords vs. paragraphs), Claude Code (needs file paths, not vague descriptions), GPT-Image, Cursor, Sora, and more.
prompt:
I want to [TASK] using [TOOL NAME].

Before writing the prompt:
1. Identify which tool I'm targeting
2. Look up the current best practices for that tool's prompt format
3. Restructure my request using that tool's ideal format
4. Flag any patterns that would waste credits or produce weak output

Then give me the optimized prompt ready to paste.

title: Track How Fast ChatGPT and Claude Cite Your Content
summary: For the first time, there are public benchmarks on how long it takes a newly published page to show up as a citation inside ChatGPT or Claude. Key benchmarks (from Josh Blyskal's analysis of billions of logs + ~900 freshly published page…
what:
For the first time, there are public benchmarks on how long it takes a newly published page to show up as a citation inside ChatGPT or Claude.

Key benchmarks (from Josh Blyskal's analysis of billions of logs + ~900 freshly published pages):
- **Median time to first citation: 6.81 days**
- 75% of pages cited within **18.68 days**
- 90% cited within **37.10 days**

If you're past day 37 without a citation, the problem is almost certainly your setup (robots.txt blocks, missing sitemap entries, page buried too deep) — not patience.
why: AEO (Answer Engine Optimization) is the new SEO. This prompt uses the AI itself to audit whether your content is structured for AI retrieval — catching robots.txt issues, sitemap gaps, and structural problems that slow citation.
how:
1. Copy the prompt template above
2. Replace `[paste here]` with your page URL
3. Paste into Claude or ChatGPT with web search enabled
4. Use the output to fix citation blockers before day 37
prompt:
You are an AEO auditor (answer engine optimization for ChatGPT, Claude, and Perplexity).
I'll paste a URL. For that page, return:
1. Likelihood of getting cited by ChatGPT or Claude within 7 days (high / medium / low) and why, using web search for the top AEO best practices as of today's data.
2. The 3 specific fixes most likely to improve citation speed.
3. The 5 query patterns this page should win as a citation in.
Be specific. Don't restate the page's content; analyze whether it's structured for retrieval.
URL: [paste here]

title: Turn Chrome Into Your AI Research Assistant for Any Logged-In Site
summary: AI agents that browse the open web are useful, but they can't reach the half of the internet that actually has your stuff: your CRM, admin console, gated databases, internal wikis. The agent gets to the login screen and stops.
what:
AI agents that browse the open web are useful, but they can't reach the half of the internet that actually has your stuff: your CRM, admin console, gated databases, internal wikis. The agent gets to the login screen and stops. [OpenAI Codex for Chrome](https://x.com/OpenAI/status/2052480800004956323) flips that — Codex now drives background tabs in Chrome from inside a session you're already logged into.

Three things that previously required a human: deep research inside subscription/paywalled sites, large-scale data transfer into your CRM or CMS, and repetitive admin-console workflows.

Note: currently unavailable in the EU and UK; requires a Codex subscription.
why: Codex doesn't take over your active browser. It opens a separate tab group, does the work, and cleans up after itself. The key constraint — "tell me what you found before clicking anything that writes data" — means you maintain a review gate before any destructive action. The tab group isolation means the agent's work stays visually separate from your own browsing.
how:
**Three workflows worth trying first:**

1. **Research inside a logged-in site:** Ask Codex to pull every article matching a topic from a paid news subscription, summarize each, and dump the summaries into a doc.

2. **CRM/CMS data transfer:** Give it a list of records to update across systems and let it click through.

3. **Admin-console automation:** Repetitive permission grants, user provisioning, settings updates that used to mean clicking through a console for an hour.

**Setup:** You need a Codex subscription and Chrome on Mac or Windows. Codex opens separate tab groups for its work, so you can keep using Chrome normally while the agent runs.
prompt:
You're driving Chrome on my behalf. Open the tabs needed for [task description].
Work entirely inside [target site] where I'm already logged in.
For each step, tell me what you found before clicking anything that writes data.
When done, close the tabs you opened and summarize what you did.

title: Turn Claude Into Your Full-Time Content Team
summary: Four content workflows that actually move the needle, from Modern Millie's tutorial: 1. Free profile audit: Install the Claude Chrome extension, open your Instagram or YouTube Studio analytics, then ask Claude to audit your profile — it…
what:
Four content workflows that actually move the needle, from Modern Millie's tutorial:

1. **Free profile audit**: Install the Claude Chrome extension, open your Instagram or YouTube Studio analytics, then ask Claude to audit your profile — it reads the live page and gives you hook strength, content gaps, and growth opportunities
