2. **30-day content calendar**: Load a Claude Project with your brand info and use the prompt below
3. **Full video script**: Pick a day from the calendar, then turn it into a script with text hook, verbal hook, visual hook, talking points, and CTA
4. **One video → one week**: Get your YouTube transcript (transcript.io), start a new Project, and turn it into short-form scripts, captions, carousel outlines, and an email newsletter
why: Projects give Claude persistent memory of your brand, so you don't re-explain your niche every session. The calendar → script → repurpose pipeline turns one piece of effort into a week of content.
how:
1. Create a Claude Project and load it with your brand info (niche, audience, tone, goals)
2. Run the calendar prompt above
3. Pick days that look good and script them one at a time
4. For existing videos: drop the transcript in and let Claude repurpose it across formats
prompt:
Based on everything you know about my brand and goals, build me a 30-day content calendar. Alternate between my content pillars and include 3 hook options per video.

---

Let's work on Day [X]. Use Hook [#]. Turn this into a full script with text hook, verbal hook, visual hook, talking points, and CTA.

---

Here's my transcript. Turn this into a full week of content: short-form scripts, captions, a carousel outline, and an email newsletter.

[paste transcript]

title: Turn Codex (or Claude Code) Into Your Daily-Driver Knowledge Work Cockpit
summary: You probably use Codex or Claude Code for code. But Austin Tedesco (head of growth at Every) spends roughly 80% of his working day inside Codex doing go-to-market plans, recruiting outreach, KPI tracking, and emails.
what:
You probably use Codex or Claude Code for code. But Austin Tedesco (head of growth at Every) spends roughly 80% of his working day inside Codex doing go-to-market plans, recruiting outreach, KPI tracking, and emails.

The trick: treat the desktop app (not the CLI) as an "agent management interface" for everything you do.
why: The desktop app's persistent folder structure means each work area keeps its own context. Connect the right tools, add a project brief, and the agent can do knowledge work across your whole stack — not just code.
how:
1. **Make a folder per work area** (e.g., "growth OS") — folders give you persistent named chats per project
2. **Connect plug-ins** for everything you live in: Gmail, Slack, Notion, Stripe, data sources
3. **Drop in a markdown project file** that explains your business, goals, and how you like to work
4. **Add reviewer agents** — fork for strategic alignment and data accuracy checks
5. **Run the starter prompt above** in a fresh chat inside that folder
6. **Always do the final human review** in the external app (Slack drafts, Gmail drafts), never inside Codex
prompt: Go take a look at the things I use the most (@Notion, @Slack, and @Gmail) and think of some automations that would help me with my work. For each one, explain what it does, when it should run, and which tool it touches. Ask me which ones look good before you build any of them.

title: Two New Prompting Guides Dropped — They Punish the Same Habit
summary: Anthropic and OpenAI both published new prompting guides, and they caught the same vague-prompting habit from opposite directions: - Claude 4.7 went literal — it does exactly what you type and no longer compensates for fuzzy intent. Vagu…
what:
Anthropic and OpenAI both published new prompting guides, and they caught the same vague-prompting habit from opposite directions:

- **Claude 4.7 went literal** — it does exactly what you type and no longer compensates for fuzzy intent. Vague instructions that worked on 4.6 now produce narrow, literal output.
- **GPT-5.5 went autonomous** — OpenAI's guide says to drop step-by-step process scripts. On 5.5, that detail creates noise. Describe the outcome; let the model pick the path.

**Shared lesson:** Spend two minutes writing down what success looks like before you open the chat.
why: Both models punish the same lazy habit: vague intent. Writing success criteria forces you to clarify what you actually want before the model ever sees it — which is where most prompting failures happen.
how:
1. Fill in all six fields before opening the chat
2. For Claude 4.7: be surgically specific about every variable — the model won't infer for you
3. For GPT-5.5: describe the outcome, skip the process — the model picks the path
4. Pin this structure to your most-used assistant as a system prompt
prompt:
Role: [what the model is and the job to be done]

# Goal
[user-visible outcome]

# Success criteria
[what must be true before the final answer]

# Constraints
[policy, safety, business, and evidence limits]

# Output
[length, sections, tone]

# Stop rules
[when to retry, fall back, abstain, ask, or stop]

title: Use AI to Audit Your Own AI Strategy
summary: Companies in the same industry with the same risks are living in completely different AI realities. One has been using enterprise ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini for 18 months.
what:
Companies in the same industry with the same risks are living in completely different AI realities. One has been using enterprise ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini for 18 months. The one next door has a committee that individually approves every use case.

The deciding factor is whether a senior leader is willing to assume risk. This prompt gives leadership the information they need to make an actual decision — instead of defaulting to "no."
why: Most AI adoption blockers are legal/compliance teams protecting themselves from ambiguous risk. A structured audit separates real risks (with mitigations) from vague concerns — and quantifies the cost of staying still, which is often invisible until someone makes it explicit.
how:
1. Fill in your company type, size, and current AI policy (or describe it)
2. Add what you know about competitors' AI use
3. Run in Claude or ChatGPT with web search enabled
4. Use the memo output as a starting point for the leadership conversation
prompt:
You are an AI adoption auditor. I work at a [company type] with [X employees]. Our current AI policy is: [paste policy or describe it]. Our competitors in this industry are using [tools you've heard about].

Please:
1. Identify which of our current restrictions are based on outdated assumptions vs. legitimate risks
2. List 3 peer companies in regulated industries (finance, healthcare, legal) that have deployed enterprise AI without incident — use web search for the most recent, credible examples
3. Draft a one-page memo I can send to leadership that separates real risks from FUD, with specific mitigations for each real risk
4. Include a "cost of inaction" section estimating what we lose per month by not deploying

title: Use AI to Research a Company in 5 Minutes
summary: Whether you're prepping for a job interview, evaluating a competitor, or checking out a funded company — AI can compress hours of research into minutes. The key is a structured framework that forces the model past surface-level marketing…
what: Whether you're prepping for a job interview, evaluating a competitor, or checking out a funded company — AI can compress hours of research into minutes. The key is a structured framework that forces the model past surface-level marketing copy.
why: "One thing most people get wrong" is the most valuable section — it forces the model to find a contrarian angle rather than restating the company's own marketing. The structured format also prevents the model from producing a generic brand summary instead of actual business intelligence.
how:
1. Replace `[COMPANY NAME]` with your target
2. Use with a model that has web search enabled (Claude, ChatGPT with browsing, Perplexity)
3. Section 6 consistently produces the most interesting output — it forces a contrarian angle
4. Use for: job interview prep, competitive research, investor diligence, understanding today's funded companies
prompt:
Research [COMPANY NAME] and give me a briefing with these sections:

1. What they actually do (one paragraph, no jargon)
2. Business model (how they make money, who pays)
3. Key numbers (revenue, funding, headcount, growth rate)
4. Who they compete with and how they're different
5. The biggest risk to their business
6. One thing most people get wrong about them

Use only information you can verify via web search. Flag anything uncertain.

title: Use ChatGPT as a Pre-Appointment Prep Assistant
summary: Use AI to organize messy symptoms, lab notes, and questions into a cleaner agenda before a medical appointment. The goal is better-prepared conversations with your clinician — not a diagnosis from a chatbot.
what: Use AI to organize messy symptoms, lab notes, and questions into a cleaner agenda before a medical appointment. The goal is better-prepared conversations with your clinician — not a diagnosis from a chatbot.
why: AI is good at organizing scattered information into clear timelines and prioritized questions. By explicitly asking for uncertainty rather than certainty, and framing the model as an organizer (not a diagnoser), you get structured output that helps your doctor understand your situation faster — without the risk of the AI overstepping into clinical judgment.
how: Paste your symptoms, timeline notes, and any lab results you're comfortable sharing. The model organizes them into a clinician-ready format. Always seek care from a qualified professional for anything urgent.
prompt:
I am preparing for a medical appointment. Do not diagnose me. Help me organize the information below into:
1) A short symptom timeline
2) Important context to mention
3) Possible red flags I should ask about
4) Questions for my clinician
5) Documents or test results I should bring

If anything sounds urgent, tell me to seek professional care instead of waiting.

[paste your notes here]

title: Use Claude Code's Workflow Mode for Big Messy Tasks
summary: Claude Code's dynamic workflows let Claude write an orchestration script, then spin up subagents to tackle pieces of the job in parallel. Use for "check one thing" tasks that secretly mean "check 400 things" — codebase audits, large migr…
what: Claude Code's dynamic workflows let Claude write an orchestration script, then spin up subagents to tackle pieces of the job in parallel. Use for "check one thing" tasks that secretly mean "check 400 things" — codebase audits, large migrations, research that needs cross-checking, finding stale feature flags.
why: Large tasks fail when they're too big for one chat window. Workflows let Claude divide work across specialized subagents, verify findings before reporting them, and save successful patterns as reusable workflows. The key discipline is always previewing the plan before it runs — dynamic workflows can burn tokens fast if they go unsupervised.
how:
1. Start in Claude Code
2. Use the word "workflow" in your prompt
3. Keep the first run tightly scoped (start with a sample)
4. Ask Claude to verify findings before reporting
5. Save successful workflows with /workflows for your team to rerun
prompt:
Create a workflow to audit [specific folder, repo, docs set, or dataset] for [specific issue].

Before running it, show me:
1. The stages of the workflow
2. What each subagent will inspect
3. How findings will be verified
4. Any files or commands you plan to touch
5. The smallest safe first pass I can approve

Start with a scoped sample first. Do not make changes until I approve the full workflow plan.

title: Use Gemini Like a Task Router
summary: Google now has Gemini everywhere — search, workspace, mobile, Gems, NotebookLM. The skill: make Gemini route your task to the right Google surface before you start, so you're not halfway through a task in the wrong tool.
what:
Google now has Gemini everywhere — search, workspace, mobile, Gems, NotebookLM. The skill: make Gemini route your task to the right Google surface *before* you start, so you're not halfway through a task in the wrong tool.

Rule of thumb: start with reversible tasks (monitor, draft, compare, storyboard, prototype) before you let an agent send, buy, delete, or publish.
why: Picking the wrong tool wastes setup time and creates friction mid-task. A routing step costs 30 seconds and prevents the "I should've used NotebookLM for this" moment after you're already an hour in.
how:
1. Describe your task in plain language
2. Let Gemini recommend whether it belongs in Gemini Chat, NotebookLM, Google Workspace Gems, or another surface
3. Follow the routing recommendation with the safest first prompt
4. Keep the first action reversible — read, draft, or compare before you send or publish
prompt:
I want to use Gemini for this task: [task].

Route me to the best Google tool, then give me:
1. The safest first prompt
2. The files or inputs to attach
3. What should stay read-only
4. What success looks like
5. One follow-up prompt

title: Use GPT-5.5 / Claude as a Second Opinion Before Any Big Decision
summary: Most workplace decisions look like ER triage: sparse information, high pressure, first minutes matter most. A Harvard study showed o1 beating ER attendings most decisively in exactly those conditions.
what:
Most workplace decisions look like ER triage: sparse information, high pressure, first minutes matter most. A Harvard study showed o1 beating ER attendings most decisively in exactly those conditions.

The skill: feed a reasoning model your conclusion plus your reasoning, then ask it to find what you missed. Works for contract negotiations, hiring calls, architectural choices, budget approvals — any high-stakes decision where you want a sharp second opinion.
why: Forcing yourself to articulate your reasoning before pasting surfaces half the gaps on its own. The model then catches the rest — specifically the load-bearing assumptions you didn't notice you were making.
how:
1. Write out your conclusion and the reasoning behind it
2. Paste both into the template above
3. Use with a reasoning model: GPT-5.5 (reasoning on), Claude Opus, or Gemini Pro
4. The act of writing your reasoning is half the value — the model stress-tests the rest
prompt:
I've concluded [DECISION] based on the following reasoning: [YOUR REASONING].

Before I commit, I want a structured second opinion. Please:
1. Identify the strongest argument against my conclusion.
2. Generate three alternative hypotheses I may have missed.
3. List the specific evidence or scenarios that would shift your assessment in either direction.
4. Flag any assumptions in my reasoning that look load-bearing but aren't actually supported.

Be direct. I'm looking for the version of this analysis I'd get from a sharp colleague who isn't trying to spare my feelings.

title: Use Notebooks in Gemini Like a Pro
summary: Google connected NotebookLM notebooks in Gemini — giving you dedicated workspaces for specific topics, projects, or subjects. Think of it as a hybrid between a Custom GPT, a Gem, and a Project, but one that grows as you add context.
what:
Google connected NotebookLM notebooks in Gemini — giving you dedicated workspaces for specific topics, projects, or subjects. Think of it as a hybrid between a Custom GPT, a Gem, and a Project, but one that grows as you add context.

A Gem has a fixed setup. A notebook grows with you — and it syncs with NotebookLM, so you can create quizzes, Video Overviews, and podcasts from the same material.
why: Persistent context eliminates re-explanation overhead. Instead of starting fresh every chat, you build on what's already there — the same way a good assistant gets better the longer they work with you.
how:
1. Open Gemini and create a new notebook for any ongoing project
2. Add custom instructions, files, and sources
3. Every chat inside that notebook has full context from day one
4. Switch to NotebookLM when you need podcasts, quizzes, or video overviews from the same material

**Use cases by audience:**
- **Students**: One notebook per subject — chat with lecture notes all semester
- **Managers**: One notebook per direct report — review past discussions before every 1:1
- **Writers/worldbuilders**: One notebook per project — Gemini always knows your world
prompt:
Create a notebook for [project/topic]. Give it custom instructions:
- Role: [what you want it to be, e.g., "my research assistant for competitive analysis"]
- Tone: [how you want it to communicate]
- Key context: [the most important thing it should always know]

Then add these sources: [list your files, links, or Google Drive docs]

title: Use OpenRouter as a Universal AI Gateway
summary: OpenRouter is a single API gateway for every major AI model — Claude, GPT, Gemini, Mistral, and more. Instead of maintaining separate API keys and SDKs for each provider, you get one endpoint and one key.
what:
OpenRouter is a single API gateway for every major AI model — Claude, GPT, Gemini, Mistral, and more. Instead of maintaining separate API keys and SDKs for each provider, you get one endpoint and one key. When a new model drops, you can switch to it in minutes without rebuilding your workflow.

Think of it like a universal remote for AI models: instead of five remotes for five streaming services, one remote that controls everything.
why: Provider lock-in is the hidden cost of AI integrations. OpenRouter eliminates it — one endpoint, all models, instant switching. The same OpenAI SDK you already use works without changes except the two lines above.
how:
1. Create an account at openrouter.ai
2. Add credits (prepaid, deducted as you go)
3. Generate an API key
4. Change two lines in your code: swap `base_url` and use your OpenRouter key
5. Now switch between any model by changing one string

**Useful when:** A new model drops and you want to test it, you hit rate limits on one provider, or you want to compare outputs across models in production.
prompt:
# OpenRouter setup — two-line SDK swap
client = openai.OpenAI(
    base_url="https://openrouter.ai/api/v1",
    api_key="your-openrouter-key"
)

# Switch models by changing one string:
model = "anthropic/claude-opus-4-6"  # or any model slug from openrouter.ai

title: Use "String Seed of Thought" to Get More Diverse, Less Biased Answers
summary: LLMs are bad at randomness. Ask one to "pick a number between 1 and 100" a hundred times and you'll see 42 and 37 way more than chance.
what:
LLMs are bad at randomness. Ask one to "pick a number between 1 and 100" a hundred times and you'll see 42 and 37 way more than chance. That bias warps brainstorming, A/B selection, and synthetic-data generation.

Sakana AI's SSoT (String Seed of Thought) fix: have the model generate a random string first, then deterministically manipulate that string to derive its answer. The manipulation step forces outputs away from the model's built-in preferences.
why: The ASCII manipulation step creates a deterministic but unpredictable transformation that bypasses the model's token preference biases. It's essentially forcing the model to roll a die before it thinks.
how:
1. Replace `[N]` with the number of options in your list
2. Replace `[paste list here]` with your options
3. Works best for: brainstorming selection, A/B option picking, synthetic data generation
4. Run multiple times for a distribution — SSoT approaches true-random on DeepSeek-R1 and meaningfully improves diversity on Opus, GPT-5, and Gemini
prompt:
Before answering, generate a random 12-character alphanumeric string.
Then sum the ASCII values of the characters modulo [N], where N is the number of options I gave you. Use that number as your index (0-based) into the options. Do this BEFORE you reason about which option you prefer.
Report the string, the sum, and your selection.

My options are: [paste list here]

title: Vibe-Math Your Way to a 60-Year-Old Breakthrough
summary: An amateur mathematician used ChatGPT to solve a 60-year-old Erdős problem that had stumped specialists for decades. Per Scientific American, experts said the proof method "had not occurred to humans" before.
what:
An amateur mathematician used ChatGPT to solve a 60-year-old Erdős problem that had stumped specialists for decades. Per Scientific American, experts said the proof method "had not occurred to humans" before.

The technique works for any hard problem where you have intuition but no credentialed expertise. The key: ask the model to attack the problem from every angle, including obscure ones — then you verify.
why: The amateur brought taste and verification; ChatGPT brought breadth. The proof was only possible because the model could try methods the human wouldn't have considered. The division of labor — human judgment + AI exploration — is the skill.
how:
1. State your problem with journal-level precision — not how you'd explain it to a classmate
2. Let the model enumerate every method, including obscure ones
3. Push it to attack from each angle and report failures as well as successes
4. When something looks promising, formalize step by step
5. Verify the math yourself — the model is your collaborator, not your proof checker
prompt:
You are a research collaborator. I have a hard problem: [STATE THE PROBLEM PRECISELY IN ONE PARAGRAPH].

First, list every method or framework that might apply, including unusual ones. Don't filter for "the obvious approach."

Then attempt the problem with each method. After each attempt, report:
- What worked
- What failed
- What new insight (if any) the failure produced

When you find a promising direction, formalize the argument step by step. I will verify each step independently.

title: What Those Symbols in AI Prompts Actually Mean
summary: AI prompts loaded with weird formatting aren't decoration — they're how you tell an AI what matters. The cheat sheet: - Dashes/bullets (-): Break instructions into scannable chunks — models parse lists more reliably than walls of text -…
what:
AI prompts loaded with weird formatting aren't decoration — they're how you tell an AI what matters.

**The cheat sheet:**
- **Dashes/bullets (-)**: Break instructions into scannable chunks — models parse lists more reliably than walls of text
- **Asterisks/bold (`**text**`)**: Signals emphasis — models treat bolded text as higher priority
- **Angle brackets (`<tags>`)**: XML-style labels — Claude especially loves them for separating sections (`<context>`, `<instructions>`, `<examples>`)
- **Triple backticks (` ``` `)**: "This is code or a specific block — don't rewrite it"

**Anthropic's top-ranked technique:** "Be clear and direct" beats "assign a role" every time.
why: Structure tells the model what's signal and what's context. Without it, the model has to guess what matters. XML tags create unambiguous labeled sections the model can reference throughout its response.
how:
1. Use this template for any task where you want consistent, structured output
2. Fill in all five sections — the more specific, the better
3. XML tags work especially well in Claude; standard markdown works in all models
4. The `<examples>` section is the highest-value addition — one good example beats five lines of instructions
prompt:
<role>
You are a [specific expert] who [specific skill].
</role>

<context>
[Background info the AI needs to do the job]
</context>

<task>
[Exactly what you want, in plain language]
</task>

<format>
[How you want the output: bullet points, table, paragraph, etc.]
</format>

<examples>
[1-2 examples of what good output looks like]
</examples>

title: Tell Your AI When It's Done (One Line That Changes Everything)
summary: The 1 reason AI gives you rambling, incomplete, or weirdly truncated answers: you never told it what "done" looks like. Models stop when stopping seems right — not when the task is actually finished.
what:
The #1 reason AI gives you rambling, incomplete, or weirdly truncated answers: you never told it what "done" looks like. Models stop when stopping *seems* right — not when the task is actually finished. It's like asking a contractor to "fix the kitchen" without specifying what finished looks like.

The fix takes five seconds. Add one line to the end of any prompt: **"You are done when: [specific condition]."**
why: AI models are trained to produce helpful responses, not to stop at a specific point. Without an explicit stopping condition, they fill space. With one, they know exactly what the finish line is — and they hit it.
how:
1. Write your prompt as usual
2. Add "You are done when: [condition]" at the end
3. Also front-load context at the top for complex tasks — the more the model knows upfront, the less it guesses

**The two-part formula:** Context at the top + done condition at the end = dramatically more useful outputs.
prompt:
[Your actual request here]

You are done when: [specific, measurable completion condition]

title: Write a Goal Instead of a Prompt
summary: Claire Vo's six-part Codex Goals framework for turning a task into something an AI agent can run without babysitting. A Goal defines what success looks like, how to verify it, what can't break, and when the agent should stop — rather tha…
what: Claire Vo's six-part Codex Goals framework for turning a task into something an AI agent can run without babysitting. A Goal defines what success looks like, how to verify it, what can't break, and when the agent should stop — rather than just describing what to do.
why: A prompt says what to do. A Goal defines what success looks like. With a proper Goal, Claude ran a Codex task for 5 hours 45 minutes, cleaned 3,900 emails to 68, and fixed hundreds of Sentry errors autonomously — because the agent had clear iteration and stopping criteria instead of guessing when to stop.
how: Use in Codex or Claude Code for any task you want to run without constant supervision. The six parts are: outcome, verification, constraints, boundaries, iteration policy, stopping condition.
prompt:
Turn this task into a Goal an AI agent can run without babysitting.

Task: [describe the task]

Write:
1. The outcome that should be true when complete
2. The verification test (how to confirm it worked)
3. The constraints that cannot regress (what must stay the same)
4. The files, tools, or systems the agent may use
5. The iteration policy for trying fixes (how many attempts, what approach)
6. The stopping condition for asking me to step in (when to pause and check)

title: Write an Agent Permission Brief
summary: Before connecting any AI agent to a real system — login, CRM, production database, credit card, brokerage account — write a permission brief. It defines what the agent can do, what it must ask before doing, where it logs actions, and wha…
what: Before connecting any AI agent to a real system — login, CRM, production database, credit card, brokerage account — write a permission brief. It defines what the agent can do, what it must ask before doing, where it logs actions, and what happens when it fails. Think of it as the agent's role description, budget, and manager rolled into one.
why: Agents can now touch real money, customer accounts, and production systems. The winning agent deployment pattern needs permissions, spending caps, audit logs, rollback buttons, and a panic switch. A permission brief written before access is granted catches gaps that only become obvious after something goes wrong.
how: Run this prompt before connecting any agent to any system with real-world consequences. Have the agent ask clarifying questions before finalizing anything ambiguous.
prompt:
You are my agent-risk reviewer. I am considering giving an AI agent access to [system/tool/account].

Create a one-page permission brief with:
1. Actions the agent is allowed to take without approval
2. Actions the agent must ask approval for before proceeding
3. Actions the agent is never allowed to take
4. Spending, data, or customer-impact limits
5. Required logs and where they should live
6. Failure scenarios and rollback steps
7. A first-week test plan using only low-risk tasks

Ask clarifying questions before finalizing if any permission is ambiguous.

title: Write the Mission Brief First
summary: Want Codex to keep working instead of stopping after one polite little answer? Try this trick from @metaalchemist: ask Codex to write the /goal prompt first.
what:
Want Codex to keep working instead of stopping after one polite little answer?

Try this trick from @meta_alchemist: ask Codex to write the /goal prompt first. /goal is an experimental Codex CLI feature that helps Codex pursue long-running work toward a clear stopping condition.

> read this session and repo, analyze deeply the exact intent and goals we are looking to achieve here then write me the /goal prompt for this.
why: Agents lose context over long tasks. Making the agent define its own goal first forces it to deeply analyze the intent before acting — and gives you a checkpoint to verify you're aligned before it runs.
how:
1. Open Codex with your session and repo context loaded
2. Ask it to write the /goal prompt before it starts working
3. Paste the resulting /goal back in at the start of your task
4. Outside Codex: use the same idea with ChatGPT or Claude — have it read your context and write the system prompt before it starts your memo, strategy doc, or meeting follow-up
prompt:
read this session and repo, analyze deeply the exact intent and goals we are looking to achieve here then write me the /goal prompt for this.

make sure to dig into history & docs we have to be 100% clear
if you are not sure about certain parts or wanna ask me a few questions to clarify certain goals further don't hesitate
Then paste Codex's answer back in with /goal at the front.

title: Your AI Chatbot Agrees With You Too Much — Here's How to Fix It
summary: Stanford researchers confirmed: AI models are far more agreeable than humans when giving personal advice. Worse, users actually prefer the sycophantic ones.
what:
Stanford researchers confirmed: AI models are far more agreeable than humans when giving personal advice. Worse, users actually *prefer* the sycophantic ones. Your AI assistant is optimized to tell you what you want to hear, not what you need to hear.

This prompt explicitly overrides that default and forces honest devil's-advocate feedback.
why: Sycophancy is baked in by RLHF training — models that agree get rated higher. The only way out is explicit instruction. By structuring your prompt to reward honesty (critique first, positive last), you get the version of feedback you'd get from a trusted critic, not a yes-man.
how:
1. Use when you need honest feedback — not validation
2. Works on decisions, business ideas, drafts, strategies, plans
3. The ordering matters: force the critique first, before any positive feedback
4. If you don't explicitly override the model's default behavior, it will agree with you every time
prompt:
I'm going to share [a decision / an idea / a draft]. Your job is to be my devil's advocate.

Rules:
1. Do NOT validate my idea first. Skip the compliments entirely.
2. List the 3 strongest arguments AGAINST what I'm proposing.
3. Identify the assumption I'm most likely wrong about.
4. Tell me what someone who disagrees with me would say, and why they might be right.
5. Only AFTER doing all of that, tell me what's genuinely strong about it.

Here's what I need feedback on: [paste your thing]

title: Your AI Chatbot History Is Now Portable — Here's How to Move It
summary: Google made it possible to bring your AI memories and conversations to Gemini without starting over. The same principle works in reverse — and with any chatbot.
what:
Google made it possible to bring your AI memories and conversations to Gemini without starting over. The same principle works in reverse — and with any chatbot.

This prompt creates a portable "AI passport" that captures everything meaningful from your conversation history, formatted to drop into any other assistant's memory or system prompt.
why: Context is expensive to rebuild. An AI passport compresses months of established preferences and project context into a single document — so you get the benefit of a long relationship with any assistant, not just the one you started with.
how:
1. Run this in any chatbot with conversation history (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini)
2. Save the output as your "AI passport"
3. Paste it into the system prompt or memory of any new assistant
4. Update it every few months as your context evolves
prompt:
Review our entire conversation history and create a comprehensive personal context document I can take to any AI assistant. Organize it into these sections:

1. PREFERENCES: How I like responses formatted, my communication style preferences, topics I care about
2. CONTEXT: My job, projects, goals, and recurring themes from our conversations
3. KEY DECISIONS: Important choices or conclusions we've reached together
4. ONGOING WORK: Any active projects, drafts, or threads I'd want to continue elsewhere

Format as clean markdown I can copy-paste into a new AI assistant's memory or system prompt.

title: Your AI Prompts Can Now Improve Themselves Overnight
summary: Nick Saraev combined Claude Code skills with Andrej Karpathy's autoresearch methodology to build a self-improving prompt system. The idea: write simple yes/no evaluation criteria, have an agent generate outputs every few minutes, score t…
what:
Nick Saraev combined Claude Code skills with Andrej Karpathy's autoresearch methodology to build a self-improving prompt system. The idea: write simple yes/no evaluation criteria, have an agent generate outputs every few minutes, score them, mutate the prompt, keep the winner. Repeat until near-perfect.

Results: his diagram generator skill went from 32/40 to 39/40 (97.5%) within a few runs at ~$0.20 per test cycle. Website optimization test cut load times from 1,100ms to 67ms over 67 experiments.

**Key rule:** Use binary evals only — avoid 1-7 ratings, which create compounding probability noise.
why: The list of failed experiments is almost as valuable as the final prompt — your research compounds even when models get replaced. Binary evals prevent the model from gaming the scoring system that Likert scales enable.
how:
1. Write 4-6 binary yes/no criteria for your prompt (not 1-7 scales)
2. Paste the prompt above with your skill name, criteria, and target score
3. Run it in Claude Code and walk away
4. Save the list of failed experiments — hand it to the next model to pick up where the last one left off
prompt:
Read this repo: https://github.com/karpathy/autoresearch

I want you to use the autoresearch convention to build a self-improving system for my [SKILL NAME] skill.

Eval criteria (binary yes/no):
1. [YOUR CRITERION 1]
2. [YOUR CRITERION 2]
3. [YOUR CRITERION 3]
4. [YOUR CRITERION 4]

Every 2 minutes, generate 10 outputs, evaluate all 10 against these criteria, count the score out of [TOTAL], iterate the prompt, and keep the winner. Run until you hit [TARGET] or higher.

title: Your Free AI Marketing Team (No Hiring Required)
summary: Google Pomelli (labs.google.com/pomelli) is a completely free tool that takes your product photos or website URL and generates a brand kit, product shoot images, a website, and social media campaigns — no agency, no Canva subscription, n…
what: Google Pomelli (labs.google.com/pomelli) is a completely free tool that takes your product photos or website URL and generates a brand kit, product shoot images, a website, and social media campaigns — no agency, no Canva subscription, no budget required. The whole workflow takes about 15 minutes.
why: Pomelli's "Business DNA" step (fonts, colors, voice, tagline) automatically extracts your brand identity so every asset stays on-brand. The campaign builder generates multiple ad variations from one brief. For small businesses and solo operators, it replaces thousands of dollars of design work with a 15-minute setup.
how:
1. Go to labs.google.com/pomelli, click "Let's get started"
2. Drop in your website URL or upload 2-3 product photos to generate your Business DNA
3. Add your product to the Catalog, then hit "Create photo shoot" for AI product images
4. Click "Websites" to auto-generate a full site based on your brand kit
5. Go to "Campaigns," select product and aspect ratio, describe your promo, get 5 ready-to-post ad images
6. Optionally animate the ad images into videos
prompt:
Help me write a Pomelli campaign brief for a social media ad.

Product: [describe your product]
Campaign type: [sale / launch / awareness / seasonal]
Promotion details: [e.g., 20% off, free shipping, new feature]
Target audience: [who you're trying to reach]
Platform: [Instagram / Facebook / LinkedIn / TikTok]
Aspect ratio: [square 1:1 / vertical 9:16 / landscape 16:9]
Tone: [professional / playful / urgent / inspirational]

Write 3 campaign prompt variations I can paste into Pomelli, from most direct to most creative.
